posts.summary: Yegge explains why he's reluctantly starting a blog despite hating the blog format. He critiques blogs for their chronological ordering, lack of threaded and inline comments, no version history, and poor content editing tools. After considering building his own solution in Ruby on Rails but realizing it would be another multi-year project like his 7-year game development effort, he pragmatically chooses Blogger. The post serves as both a manifesto for better self-publishing software and an explanation for why he's settling for imperfect tools.
key_points: Blogs should organize content by popularity, not chronology, with inline comments that grow 'outward' from specific sentences rather than 'downward' at the end
What's needed is an 'essay-publishing system' that combines blog, wiki, and social voting features with version history for posts and comments
Web programming is still a 'Big Crap Sandwich' and even Rails can't make ambitious solo projects feasible for people with day jobs
His 7-year game project taught him that ideas vastly outnumber available time, and he can't chase every 5-year idea anymore
Java's lack of dynamic development capabilities forced him to rewrite half his game in Jython - dynamic languages are essential for extensible applications
money_quotes: Blog entries should be organized by popularity, not time. Or ideally, you can pick either one. Why the hell don't blogs do that?
The commentary on a blog entry should grow outward, not downward.
Web Programming is a Big Crap Sandwich, and we all have to take a bite of it.
I've realized that I have more ideas than time. Way more. It's not even close.
If I come up with ten ideas that take 5 years each, I'm pretty screwed.
18 months, what a joke of an estimate.
It simply isn't possible to do an extensible game like that in Java. You either write your own minilanguage or you use a JVM language; those are your only choices.
I can't afford to go chasing every 5-year idea that springs to mind.
posts.key_insight: The gap between our vision for how software should work and our ability to build it forces painful compromises, especially when ideas far outnumber available years.

posts.summary: Yegge argues that programmers should learn math, but not the math taught in schools. After spending 15 months relearning mathematics, he discovered that the curriculum (calculus, trig, geometry) is poorly suited for programmers, who actually need discrete math, probability, statistics, and linear algebra. He advocates for a breadth-first, Wikipedia-surfing approach to learning math rather than grinding through exercises, emphasizing that programming experience makes math much easier to pick up.
key_points: Schools teach the wrong math for programmers—continuous math (calculus, trig) when we mostly need discrete math, probability, and linear algebra
Math is much easier to learn after you know how to program, because programming gives you intuition for functions, algorithms, and formal systems
The right way to learn math is breadth-first: survey the field, learn what things are called, understand when to apply them—then look up details as needed
Problem identification is the first step—if you don't know what 'combinatorics' or 'convex optimization' are, you can't recognize when you need them
Mathematical notation is like programming syntax: intimidating at first, but patterns emerge and it becomes readable with exposure
money_quotes: You can be a good, solid, professional programmer without knowing much math. But hey, you don't really need to know how to program, either.
Math is a lot easier to pick up after you know how to program. In fact, if you're a halfway decent programmer, you'll find it's almost a snap.
They teach math all wrong in school. Way, WAY wrong.
The right way to learn math is breadth-first, not depth-first. You need to survey the space, learn the names of things, figure out what's what.
The first step to applying mathematics is problem identification. If you have a problem to solve, and you have no idea where to start, it could take you a long time to figure it out.
Mathematical notation is the biggest turn-off to outsiders.
Don't let exercises put you off the math. If an exercise is starting to bore you, move on. Let your intuition guide you.
posts.key_insight: Programmers should learn math breadth-first by surveying the field and learning what tools exist, because problem identification matters more than memorizing formulas—and programming experience makes mathematical concepts surprisingly accessible.

posts.summary: Yegge responds to accusations of being a snobbish interviewer by drawing satirical parallels to other demanding professions like airline pilots and brain surgeons. He argues that top tech companies have legitimately high standards because they've been burned by lowering them, and that software engineering has fragmented into specialized roles just like medicine. The post acknowledges interviewing is imperfect but defends the practice of testing candidates on computer science fundamentals, while advising candidates to research companies, vote with their feet, and improve the process from within rather than complaining anonymously online.
key_points: Tech companies test on algorithms, data structures, and CS fundamentals because engineers actually use these skills daily - lowering standards leads to costly failures
The software industry has fragmented into specializations; being good at web development doesn't qualify you for kernel work and vice versa
Interviewing is imperfect and somewhat of a 'crapshoot' but companies all do it roughly the same way
Motivation and desire to improve oneself matters more than current knowledge - candidates on an upward curve get more leeway
Big company jobs aren't as glamorous as they seem; startups offer more excitement but more risk
money_quotes: If you want a job at a company like Microsoft, Yahoo!, Apple, or Amazon.com, they're going to have high standards. It doesn't matter if you 'know how to program'.
But when they lower their standards, they get burned. The 747 crashes, the patient dies, the juggler drops a bowling pin on someone's head, the tiger rips someone's throat out.

Putting together a pretty Ruby on Rails site is no small feat. Learning to program is no small feat. Many people try and fail to get even that far. But it's 3- to 5-ball juggling, and it just doesn't cut it for the Cirque du Soleils and private hospitals of the software industry.
Our industry has fragmented, just the way the medical industry has, and there are different job roles now.
I count motivation (that is, the desire to improve oneself) higher than any other factor in an interview.
The truth about interviewing is that everyone does it their own way, and it's always about the same: people interview for what they know.
If you don't know something, don't go berserk on us. Just go read about it on Wikipedia. It's not as hard as you think!
posts.key_insight: Technical interviewing's high standards exist because software engineering is a demanding profession with real consequences for failure, and rather than complaining about it, candidates should either acquire the skills companies need or find companies whose priorities match their own.

posts.summary: Yegge argues that Moore's Law represents a dead end because we're optimizing serial computation on fundamentally flawed von Neumann architecture instead of pursuing parallel computing. He contends that programming languages have all failed because they're designed for outdated hardware, and that programmers' resistance to learning new paradigms keeps us trapped in this evolutionary dead-end. The solution requires simultaneously revolutionizing hardware, languages, data structures, and algorithms—but programmers' aversion to painful learning makes this unlikely. He concludes by encouraging continuous learning despite the pain, as the only path toward potential improvement.
key_points: All current programming languages have failed because they're fundamentally serial and optimized for von Neumann architecture
Von Neumann's original computer was a prototype built from available parts—he spent his final decade working on cellular automata as the real future of computing
True progress requires simultaneous revolution in hardware, languages, data structures, and algorithms—incremental approaches have failed
Programmers obsess over performance but refuse to learn new paradigms that could give 1000x improvements
Continuous learning is essential but painful; you must 'make time' and accept the discomfort like physical training
money_quotes: To me, mainstream languages have failed because they're still fundamentally serial, fundamentally early von Neumann, fundamentally single-box languages.
They're all vying for the position of 'least crappy', and the current winner (whether it's Python, Ruby, Lisp, name your favorite) is just that: the least crappy.
Let me give you a hint: your brain's operating system isn't written in C++.
The computer you're reading this blog rant on was his frigging prototype! He was going to throw it out and make a better one!
You have no idea the contempt I feel for C++, for J2EE, for your favorite XML parser, for the pathetic junk we're using to perform computations today.

Programmers are obsessed with performance, and they'll go to almost any length to fiddle with their algorithms and data representations... Any length, that is, except for learning a new language on new hardware.
Learning is hard. If it's easy, then you're coasting; you're not making yourself better at something fundamentally new.
We're ignoring the First Law of Holes.
posts.key_insight: The entire computing stack—hardware, languages, algorithms—must change simultaneously to escape our von Neumann dead-end, but programmers' refusal to endure the pain of learning new paradigms keeps us trapped.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge argues that Java's rigid object-oriented paradigm forces programmers to wrap every action (verb) inside a class (noun), leading to absurdly verbose code with unnecessary abstractions like AbstractProxyMediator and NotificationStrategyFactory. Using an extended allegory of 'Kingdom of Javaland,' he illustrates how this noun-centric thinking distorts natural problem-solving by requiring chaperone objects for simple operations. He contrasts Java with functional languages and even C++, which allow standalone functions as first-class citizens. The post concludes with a call for Java to add first-class functions so programmers can think and code more naturally.
key_points: Java forces all verbs (functions) to be owned by nouns (classes), creating unnecessary wrapper objects
Other languages like Python, Ruby, JavaScript, and functional languages allow standalone functions, avoiding abstraction bloat
The infamous Java naming patterns (AbstractProxyMediatorFactory) are symptoms of forced noun-orientation, not good architecture
C++ despite its flaws provides more expressive flexibility by supporting standalone functions and proper namespaces
Java could fix this by adding first-class functions without breaking its class-based conventions
money_quotes: In Javaland, by King Java's royal decree, Verbs are owned by Nouns.
Advocating Object-Oriented Programming is like advocating Pants-Oriented Clothing.
Dude, not everything is an object.

Classes are really the only modeling tool Java provides you. So whenever a new idea occurs to you, you have to sculpt it or wrap it or smash at it until it becomes a thing, even if it began life as an action, a process, or any other non-thing concept.
You'll almost never find an AbstractProxyMediator, a NotificationStrategyFactory, or any of their ilk in Python or Ruby.
Need to wait? Waiter.execute(). Brush your teeth? ToothBrusher(myTeeth).go(). Take out the garbage? TrashDisposalPlanExecutor.doIt().
The range of succinctly expressible thoughts far exceeds Java's, because C++ gives you verbs, and who'd want to speak in a language that doesn't?
posts.key_insight: Java's insistence that every function must belong to a class forces programmers to create absurd noun hierarchies that obscure simple actions, when first-class functions would let code mirror natural thought.

posts.summary: Yegge argues that despite Lisp's legendary status and theoretical elegance, no current implementation is actually acceptable for practical use. He identifies four major problems: fragmentation across incompatible dialects (Scheme vs Common Lisp vs various implementations), an outdated specification lacking modern features like threading and Unicode, CLOS being a bolted-on object system that doesn't achieve true OOP seamlessness, and macros that while powerful are poorly supported by tools and remain second-class citizens. He notes that Paul Graham's Arc vaporware has paradoxically hurt Lisp adoption by making programmers wait rather than commit to existing options. Yegge concludes that Lisp needs a revolution with a benevolent dictator, a new name, and lessons learned from 45 years of language evolution.
key_points: The 'which Lisp?' problem kills adoption - newcomers must choose between Scheme and Common Lisp, then pick specific implementations, with no canonical version like Python or Ruby has
Paul Graham's Arc announcement 'Microsofted Lisp' by creating vaporware that made people wait rather than adopt existing implementations
CLOS was bolted onto Common Lisp rather than designed from the ground up, leaving visible seams like length() not being polymorphic
Macros are powerful but remain second-class citizens with poor tooling support, no portable hygiene options, and are treated as a last resort rather than a first-class feature
The Common Lisp spec is ancient and missing critical modern features like threads, Unicode, and GUI support - and political infighting prevents updates
money_quotes: If Lisp were acceptable, then we'd all be using it.
Lisp is just the last semi-civilized outpost you hit before it turns into a dirt road, one that leads into the godawful swamp most of us spend our programming careers slugging around in.
A language is always either gaining or losing ground, and Lisp was losing ground all through the 1990s.
By deciding to pre-announce Arc, he Microsofted Lisp. Killed it with vaporware.
Religions dislike each other, but no heretic is as damned as someone who starts with your religion and makes a modification to it.
Macros are like having these high-powered band-aids, when what you want is not to be wounded in the first place.
Having the object system — something pretty fundamental to the language, you'd think — written as a bunch of macros doesn't feel right when all is said and done.
Lisp needs a benevolent dictator. Lisp needs to ditch the name 'Lisp', since it scares people.
posts.key_insight: Lisp's theoretical elegance is undermined by practical fragmentation, outdated specifications, and bolted-on systems that prevent it from ever achieving the seamless experience of modern languages.

posts.summary: Yegge argues that the software industry is trapped in religious-like tribalism around programming languages, where rational discourse is impossible because developers treat their preferred languages as sacred objects. He draws an extended parallel between programming language communities and organized religion, complete with popes, hierarchies, and the persecution of heretics. The essay was prompted by hostile reactions to his previous criticism of Lisp, which he uses as evidence that even supposedly enlightened technical communities are capable of the same irrational zealotry that characterized the Dark Ages. He calls for philosopher-like thinkers to emerge in software who can challenge cherished beliefs and move the industry forward through rational inquiry rather than tribal warfare.
key_points: Programming languages function as religions with deities (the language), popes (language designers), hierarchies (core contributors to newcomers), and persecution of heretics who question orthodoxy
The hostile reaction to any criticism of a programming language reveals that developers are 'religious fanatics' incapable of rational discourse about their tools
Philosophers historically freed humanity from religious mind-control, and software needs similar thinkers to free us from technological tribalism
The author abandoned Common Lisp not for technical reasons alone, but because the community's religious fervor made rational discussion of its flaws impossible
We're in a 'Software Dark Ages' where progress is hindered by emotional investment in existing tools rather than honest evaluation of their limitations
money_quotes: It was philosophers that got us out of that Dark Ages mess, and no small number of them lost their lives in doing so. And today, the philosophy majors are the butts of the most jokes, because after the philosophers succeeded in opening our minds, we forgot why we needed them.
Everyone who commented angrily on that blog entry was caught. I caught you, anonymous or not, being a religious fanatic.

I'm taking a stand, all right, and it's a very definite one. I'm finding myself drawn inexorably towards a single goal: stamping out technological religion, because I'm frigging tired of not being able to stick to the facts.
I much prefer Lisp the idea to Lisp the implementation.

The lesson is that they are you. Whenever you hear someone ranting about something you take for granted as wonderful and praiseworthy, and you're wondering why they don't leave well enough alone so we can all get back to our incestuous cheerleading, just remember: we went from the Dark Ages to our reeeeasonably enlightened society today by questioning our most cherished beliefs.

You'd think your rank would be accrued by virtue of your technical and/or documentation contributions, but in practice it's usually more of a function of how many converts you've gained, how many followers you have, how much you've been spreading the Word.
posts.key_insight: The software industry's religious attachment to programming languages prevents the rational discourse needed for genuine progress, and we need philosopher-like critics willing to challenge sacred cows despite the inevitable backlash.

posts.summary: This deeply personal essay is a departure from Yegge's usual technical blogging, centered on his brother Dave who died of lymphoma at age 23 after being misdiagnosed. Yegge reflects on how Dave taught him to write, to have a sense of humor, and to live fully. He reveals that for four years after Dave's death, he thought about suicide every night. The post serves as both an elegy and an explanation for why Yegge writes—he's writing for Dave. He announces his intention to broaden his blog beyond technical topics, while acknowledging readers' expectations, and asks that this particular post not be shared on Reddit or Digg.
key_points: Dave was misdiagnosed twice with bronchitis before doctors discovered advanced lymphoma—a tumor between his heart and lungs
Dave maintained his legendary sense of humor until the end, joking about losing bowel control by referencing Trainspotting
Yegge experienced suicidal ideation every night for four years after Dave's death
The experience gave Yegge a different perspective on life and criticism: 'My brother was tortured to death' puts technical debates in context
Yegge writes not for his audience but for Dave, and wants to expand beyond technical topics
money_quotes: I've been dead ever since then, you know. I didn't close my eyes for a single night, for four years after he died, without thinking of offing myself, because I missed him so much.
He was a good Liver, to coin an utterly awful phrase; he lived, and that's always better than writing.
Writing just tries to show you how people lived, and it never comes close, never gives you that rush of being there just when something wonderful is happening.
Look on the bright side: at least I didn't have to clean it up!
My brother was tortured to death. I'll spare you the gruesome details, but aside from the miracle of morphine, those folks in medieval torture chambers had nothing on him.
I just try to make people around me happy, and enjoy myself, until, you know, I have some sort of major Trainspotting incident.
People are always broader than what they blog about, but we sort of expect the best bloggers to stay on topic.
posts.key_insight: Living fully and making people laugh matters more than anything you could ever write about, because you never know how long it'll last.

posts.summary: Yegge reviews The Elder Scrolls IV: Oblivion, a massively addictive single-player RPG that he's been playing instead of blogging. Despite frequent crashes (which he attributes to C++), ugly NPC faces, and repetitive voice acting, he finds the game exceptional due to its beautiful scenery, smart monster AI, and immersive single-player experience. He argues that single-player games have distinct advantages over MMORPGs because they're focused entirely on the player and don't require dealing with toxic multiplayer communities or subscription fees.
key_points: Oblivion is so addictive that real life starts looking like a graphics engine, and players develop genuine aversion to reality
The game crashes frequently, which Yegge uses as evidence that C++ remains problematic for game development
Despite improved graphics, NPCs are disturbingly ugly with realistic but unflattering facial details, and voice acting is limited and melodramatic
Monster AI is genuinely impressive - enemies use evasion tactics, chase players across zones, and will fight other NPCs or monsters
Single-player RPGs have inherent advantages over MMORPGs: no subscriptions, no toxic players, actual plot, and the story is about YOU
money_quotes: The game is so damned addictive that when you're not playing, you wind up looking outside and thinking 'man, that lighting model is really realistic!' before you remember you're not actually playing at that particular moment.
Bethesda called this latest incarnation 'Oblivion' because it sounded better than 'core dump' or 'segmentation fault', but it has the same basic connotations.
Crashes? *gasp* -- I bet you'll never guess what programming language it's written in.
Morrowind was a hideous island dominated by volcanoes and disease storms and mud flats and rotting undead zombies, much like a C++ users convention.
The game's supposed to be an escape from reality, but at times it feels more like an escape to Arkansas.
When you hear an NPC talking about the exploits of some hero, you know it's about you. You don't have to deal with all the assholes you run into in multiplayer games.
Oblivion is a crashy game. It's a case study in why the world shouldn't be using C++.
I highly, nay strongly recommend that you don't start, or you'll suddenly develop an aversion to Real Life, and who knows how long it'll last. Probably until you're fired.
posts.key_insight: Even deeply flawed games can be incredibly compelling when they focus on making the player the hero of their own story, which is something multiplayer games fundamentally cannot do.

posts.summary: Yegge argues that the desire to become a manager is itself a red flag, as the best managers are actually leaders who never sought management roles. He contends that empathy is the single most important trait for managing developers, capable of smoothing over nearly any mistake. The post criticizes companies that treat management as a promotion over engineering, arguing this drives away talent and pushes organizations toward mediocrity. He distinguishes sharply between management (bureaucratic simulation of leadership) and actual leadership (vision-driven inspiration), suggesting that great engineers will build great things faster without managers getting in their way.
key_points: Empathy is the 'secret sauce' of good management - if you truly empathize with your engineers, they can forgive almost any mistake
Companies that treat management as a promotion over engineering create a death spiral that pushes out their best engineers
The desire to be a manager is itself suspicious - the best managers are leaders who had management thrust upon them
Leadership and management are orthogonal: management is bureaucratic process that attempts to simulate leadership
Assume you're a bad manager by default, because most managers are average or below, and you won't improve unless you're actively looking for problems
money_quotes: If you want to manage badly enough, then you will manage, badly enough.
You should never give power to someone who craves it, for reasons that I hope are obvious.
Any time I hear someone say 'I want to be a manager', I just want to smack them.
If you take all the managers away, great engineers will still build great things. Maybe even faster.
Management is just a bureaucratic framework that attempts to simulate leadership through process and protocol.
If you don't know whether you're a bad manager, then you're a bad manager. It's the default state.
Or maybe you just want to be a little higher in the pecking order, so you can peck downhill? If so, then you're what we call, colloquially speaking, a 'pecker'.

The best engineers either leave the company or try their hand at management, often with doubly disastrous consequences: they simultaneously lose the company a great engineer and gain them an awful manager.
posts.key_insight: The catch-22 of software management is that those who want it most are usually worst at it, because true leadership comes from vision and empathy rather than desire for authority.

posts.summary: Yegge enthusiastically reviews Emacs 22's new features, focusing primarily on two game-changers: native Unicode/UTF-8 support and the ability to use Lisp expressions in replacement strings for search-and-replace operations. Through progressively complex examples, he demonstrates how the enhanced replace-regexp command transforms text manipulation from tedious manual work into powerful one-liner transformations. He concludes with advice on learning Emacs Lisp, arguing it's a worthwhile investment that won't become obsolete and recommending specific books for mastering it.
key_points: Native UTF-8/Unicode support in Emacs 22 finally makes international character handling seamless after years of awkward workarounds
The new \, syntax in replace-regexp allows arbitrary Lisp evaluation in replacements, enabling complex text transformations in a single command
Emacs can outperform refactoring IDEs for certain text transformations because it treats code as manipulable text rather than requiring semantic understanding
Emacs Lisp programming is fundamentally different from other languages - it's like having a 'worker bee' version of yourself that zips around the buffer performing actions
Learning Emacs Lisp is a worthwhile long-term investment because neither Emacs nor Emacs Lisp is going anywhere for decades
money_quotes: Even if you do most of your coding in the comfy confines of a visual IDE, it can be awfully handy to keep Emacs around for your fine-grained text surgery.
I hope by now you're beginning to suspect that knowing a little Emacs-Lisp can help you immensely with your editing tasks. Believe it!
Just try doing that exercise in Java or C++ sometime.
Clearly no fancy-pants refactoring IDE on the planet is going to be able to help you with this.
Programmers really hate new syntax. Most people find it harder to learn new syntax than to learn new Design Patterns or APIs or frameworks.
It's like you've got a little worker-bee version of yourself, doing automatically what you could have done by hand (albeit much more slowly) using editor commands.
Emacs isn't going anywhere, and Emacs-Lisp isn't going anywhere, not for several decades at least, so it will benefit you to learn them deeply. It will never be obsolete knowledge.
posts.key_insight: The combination of regular expressions and inline Lisp evaluation in Emacs 22's replace-regexp creates a text transformation power tool that surpasses specialized refactoring IDEs for many real-world editing tasks.

posts.summary: Yegge imagines a future where 'Wizard Schools' — elite seven-year boarding schools for programming prodigies — have revolutionized tech education and made traditional CS degrees nearly obsolete. Written as a retrospective from 2017, the fictional narrator describes how these Harry Potter-themed academies produce graduates who type 160 WPM, code in 'folding languages' (Lisp/Haskell derivatives), can draw, speak multiple languages, and command salaries of $250K-$1M. The post is actually a critique of current CS education and a manifesto for what programming education could become if it prioritized practical coding skills, physical fitness, arts training, and hiring brilliant industry practitioners as teachers.
key_points: Traditional CS PhD programs have become watered down — graduates often can't program and over-specialize in narrow research areas with little real-world applicability
The imagined Wizard Schools succeed by combining intensive coding practice, discrete math, physical education, arts training, and music — producing well-rounded programmers
Universities prioritize research over education, treating teaching undergrads as beneath them, which is why students graduate without knowing fundamentals like compilers and operating systems
Elite tech companies can easily spot credential-holding duds — what matters is what you know and can do, not your degree
The market craving for better programming education is enormous; people only went to college because nothing better existed
money_quotes: Some Ph.D.s can, but how many of them is it, really? From an industry perspective, an alarming number of them are no-ops.
What matters is what you *know*, and what you can *do*, and a Ph.D. laureate in CS these days, even from a 'top' university, has about an 80% chance of failing interviews at one of these companies.
While a 'normal' programmer is puzzling over design patterns, or trying to simplify complex code paths, a Wizard is blasting out code at the rate of hundreds of thousands of lines a *year*.
They call them Folding Languages because they write code that writes code that writes code...
Given that most companies can only afford a few Staff Wizards, the Wizards are usually forced to capitulate and use Java or C++.
Universities aren't really there to produce superstar programmers; their primary job is research, not education, and at least until recently they hardly focused on the students at all.
There are an awful lot of people in this world who care about improving their skills and knowledge, and they want to do it as fast as humanly possible.
posts.key_insight: CS education is fundamentally broken because universities prioritize research over teaching practical programming skills, and someone who built an intensive, practice-focused alternative could revolutionize the industry.

posts.summary: Yegge reflects on being included in a 'top 10 programmers' list despite not considering himself exceptional, concluding that fame in programming comes from writing frameworks people love or writing prose about programming—not from actual code quality. He observes that most programmers we admire are known for building extensible systems or authoring compelling technical writing, since their actual code is rarely examined and usually looks ordinary. He extends this to wealth, noting that rich programmers got that way through startups and business acumen, not coding excellence. The post is a self-deprecating meditation on what actually matters in a programming career versus what we think matters.
key_points: Programming fame comes from two sources: building frameworks/languages people use, or writing compellingly about technical topics—not from code quality itself
Most 'famous' programmers' actual code looks ordinary when examined; they succeed because they shipped something useful, not because every line was beautiful
Wealthy programmers got rich through startups, timing, and business skills—being a great coder is neither necessary nor sufficient for financial success
Impact beats perfection: shipping working code matters more than writing beautiful code that never launches
Personal skill improvement has uncertain payoffs for fame or wealth, but provides satisfaction and preparedness for opportunities
money_quotes: So what makes a programmer famous? Apparently not programming! Or at least not their actual code.
I'll pick impact. You can always fix the code later.
Some people (e.g. Richard Gabriel) have argued convincingly that writing crappy code has evolutionary advantages.
Go look at the source code for Emacs or Rails or Python or Firefox, and it's just a big ball of code. In fact, often as not it's a big hairy ball.
If your code just barely works, then it's good enough.
They do say money can't buy happiness, but every time someone's tried to buy me some happiness by giving me money, it's worked on the first try.
I'd love it even more if I were a fat old dragon sitting on a big pile of gold, because then I could buy a zillion-inch monitor and work on programs I like.
posts.key_insight: Fame and wealth in programming come not from writing great code, but from shipping useful frameworks and writing compelling prose about the craft.

posts.summary: Yegge argues that cosmetic body modification will eventually become as socially acceptable as changing clothes, but American puritanical culture currently forces people to lie about procedures they've had. He catalogs the strange inconsistencies in what's taboo (nose jobs, liposuction) versus acceptable (hair coloring, tattoos), suggesting economics will eventually drive full acceptance since attractive people receive better treatment. The essay is actually a meta-commentary on how difficult it is to introduce radical new ideas to large groups, using plastic surgery acceptance as a teaching metaphor for upcoming technical posts.
key_points: The body is transitioning from 'prison for your soul' to 'clothes for your soul' that you can customize freely
American puritanical heritage creates arbitrary taboos around cosmetic procedures that other countries have already moved past
Group intelligence decreases with size - two people are smarter than one, but fifty people act 'about as smart as a dog'
Economic incentives will drive cosmetic surgery acceptance because attractive people get better jobs and treatment
This essay is a setup for technical posts about ideas that face similar resistance from group-think
money_quotes: Your body is no longer a prison for your soul. It's become more like a house, one that you can decorate to your tastes. In the fullness of time it may even become more like clothes for your soul, and you'll change it daily.

When you put two people together, they're smarter than one person. But a group of three or four is back to being about as smart as a single person. A group of ten to twenty people acts about as smart as a lost child, and a group of fifty is only about as smart as a dog.
A group of a hundred people? A sheep, of course. A thousand or more? Lemmings. When we're in big groups, we just follow what everyone around us is doing.
Ideas embody change, and someone is always profiting from the status quo. The profiting isn't always money -- sometimes people simply have their self-image tied up in the status quo.
It takes a long time for a culture to shake off the often ridiculous ideas passed down from our forebears.
Our current ideas about the world and about ourselves can make it horribly hard to contemplate new ones, technical or not.
posts.key_insight: The real subject isn't plastic surgery but the meta-problem of how group membership and cultural inertia make people resist obviously beneficial new ideas, whether cosmetic or technical.

posts.summary: Yegge explains his month-long blogging hiatus, attributing it to a combination of factors: an unkept promise about a controversial technical topic, a work project, a vacation spent building a Ruby on Rails site, and most significantly, the psychological pressure of sudden popularity after Joel Spolsky linked to his blog. He describes the anxiety of having 70,000 readers scrutinizing his work and announces a strategy of writing shorter posts to overcome the block.
key_points: Joel Spolsky is 'the Oprah Winfrey of tech blogging' - a single link from him increased Yegge's traffic from 9,000 to 70,000 daily visitors
Ruby on Rails compared to working with other web technologies is 'like having a pillow surgically removed from your face'
Google products are 'just good enough' - Blogger, Spreadsheets, Google Pages all serve their purpose without being exceptional
Sudden blog popularity creates psychological pressure that makes it harder to write, not easier
The solution to blogger's block is to 'spew it out' - write small things to clear the backlog
money_quotes: RoR is like having a pillow surgically removed from your face. I can breathe again.
That guy is like the Oprah Winfrey of tech blogging.
Google has a way of creating things I actually use. Blogger's just good enough.
After your blog gets that popular, even for a little while, you'd better grow a thick skin fast.
Imagine all of them glaring at you. Imagine them wanting to lynch you! Some of them did!
It's the #1 rule of blogging, you know: when in doubt, spew it out.
If you say something incredibly stupid and insensitive, no big deal, everyone will just despise you.
posts.key_insight: Blogging success creates a paradox where increased audience size generates psychological pressure that makes authentic writing more difficult, not less.

posts.summary: Yegge recounts his unexpected journey into anime fandom, sparked by watching Miyazaki's Spirited Away with his wife. A chance encounter with a knowledgeable but poor Suncoast employee named Ashley led to discovering Twelve Kingdoms and Last Exile, which became obsessive favorites. After spending thousands on DVDs and rewatching series multiple times, he shares his curated top 10 list of anime series and movies, emphasizing that while 90% is crap, the good stuff is genuinely fantastic. The post serves as both a personal narrative and a recommendation guide for anime newcomers.
key_points: Finding anime you love requires sifting through a lot of mediocrity, but the gems are worth the search
Anime spans as many genres as any other medium - dismissing it as 'just cartoons' misses the point entirely
Rewatching complex anime reveals new layers each time, similar to how children process favorite movies
The expense of anime DVDs is a real consideration - the hobby can easily cost thousands
Series tend to be more immersive and rewarding than standalone films due to deeper character development
money_quotes: I can tell you this much: that girl cost us thousands of dollars over the next 18 months.

You know how little kids at a certain age like to watch the same movie over and over and over again, for up to a year, and child psychologists say that each time they see it they're seeing it from a new perspective? Well, 12 Kingdoms was like that for us.
I'm so jealous of you. You haven't watched it. What I wouldn't give to erase it from my brain so I can see it again for the first time.
Whenever some Anime-store clerk half-disparagingly tells us a series is 'a thinker', we know we'll probably like it.
If a bunch of reviewers say it was boring and they didn't understand half of it, we go check it out.
Sturgeon's Revelation applies to Anime too. 90% of it is crap.
Man, I'm jealous of Japanese people. Their television just blows ours away.
posts.key_insight: Great art exists in every medium, but finding it requires patience, trusted recommendations, and the willingness to persist through initial unfamiliarity.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge argues that JavaScript is unexpectedly the most important programming language in the world because it's the gateway to browser-based applications, which is where most users now live. Despite JavaScript's flaws and the nightmarish complexity of browser programming (with its multiple languages, ugly APIs, and Microsoft's deliberate standards sabotage), the economics of web development make it inevitable that a Rails-like simplification will emerge for the browser. Yegge also reflects on blogger's block, realizing that the pressure of public criticism was stifling his creativity, and decides to stop reading comments to recapture the creative freedom of falling-asleep thinking.
key_points: JavaScript is the most important language today because browsers are where most people live, making it the only universal client-side platform
Browser programming is a nightmare of multiple languages (JavaScript, CSS, XUL, RDF, manifests) that violates conceptual integrity - 'Hello World' in Mozilla requires 6-7 files
Microsoft deliberately sabotages web standards to maintain competitive advantage, creating a standoff that holds back innovation
A Rails-like radical simplification for browser programming is economically inevitable because companies desperately want cool web apps
Most programmers avoid web development because it's seen as 'sissy stuff' and requires learning a mess of barely-interoperable languages
money_quotes: JavaScript is probably the most important language in the world today. Funny, huh? You'd think it would be Java or C++ or something.
Microsoft has always been awful at making APIs, always always always, and I've decided over the years to credit this to malice rather than incompetence.
Nobody wants to be a web developer. No self-respecting, rootin'-tootin', core-dump-debugging programmer wants to be one, anyway. That's sissy stuff.
I'm all in favor of MVC, but I think Common Sense and its kissing-cousin Conceptual Integrity will both tell you that M, V and C don't need to be three separate languages.
It's a crap sandwich and we all have to take a bite. But it's also a greenfield opportunity, a land rush, a high-energy state just dying to become a low-energy state.
You're not still a thick-client programmer, are you? Oh dear. You'd better get yourself a DHTML book and an AJAX book, and right-quick.
Your mind is in a creative frenzy, and you mostly suppress it during the day.
posts.key_insight: Despite being universally despised by 'real' programmers, JavaScript's captive browser audience makes it the most strategically important language, and the economic pressure for simpler web development will inevitably produce a transformative framework.

posts.summary: Yegge observes Ruby's rapid rise in bookstore shelf space to rival Python, then pivots to his central thesis: Java's biggest failing is its lack of literal syntax for data structures. While Java's platform (JVM, tooling, concurrency libraries) remains excellent, the language forces programmers into verbose, Swing-style object assembly patterns for hashmaps, lists, and nested structures that other languages handle with simple literal declarations. This verbosity drives Java developers toward XML and external data formats, adding impedance mismatch and performance overhead. He concludes that Ruby's success stems from better complexity management, and Sun's move to support scripting languages on the JVM may be the right approach since Java prioritizes performance and reliability over productivity.
key_points: Ruby books have nearly caught up to Python on bookstore shelves, signaling genuine market momentum beyond publisher hype
Liking a language and using it are 'mostly orthogonal dimensions' - Yegge uses Ruby despite not deeply knowing it because it's the path of least resistance
Java's biggest failing is the lack of literal syntax for hashmaps, arrays, objects, and functions - forcing verbose OOP assembly patterns
Java's platform (JVM, concurrency libraries, tooling) raises the bar on all competitors, which is why no better language has supplanted it
Every language has a complexity ceiling determined by design decisions in the type system and syntax, not libraries - and Java is hitting its ceiling
money_quotes: Liking and using are mostly orthogonal dimensions, and if you like the language you're using even a little bit, you're lucky.
I have a lot of trouble writing about Ruby, because I find there's nothing to say. It's why I almost never post to the O'Reilly Ruby blog. Ruby seems so self-explanatory to me.
Building UIs in Swing is this huge, festering gob of object instantiations and method calls. It's OOP at its absolute worst.
Java programmers wind up dealing with this kind of thing by writing generic helper functions, and it winds up layering even more OOP overhead onto something that ought to be a simple declaration.
Libraries can make you more productive, but they have almost no effect on the scalability of the language.
Every language has a complexity ceiling, and it's determined by a whole slew of policy and design decisions within the language, not the libraries.
Java's demonstrating quite clearly that at a certain level of complexity, the libraries and frameworks start to collapse under their own weight.
Object-Oriented Programming. Touted even by skeptics as a radical leap forward in productivity, and all OOP really is boils down to a set of organizational techniques.
posts.key_insight: A language's productivity ceiling is determined by its syntax and type system design decisions, not its libraries - and Java's lack of literal syntax for common data structures forces developers into verbose patterns that scripting languages solve elegantly.

posts.summary: Yegge argues that Agile methodologies are mostly a marketing scam preying on desperate developers, but there exists a 'good agile' exemplified by Google's engineering culture. He contrasts Bad Agile—with its rigid sprints, endless meetings, and date-driven development—against Google's incentive-based, autonomous approach where engineers choose their projects and launches emerge organically. The key insight is that great software comes from removing obstacles and aligning incentives rather than adding process. Google achieves this through exceptional perks, peer recognition, and a culture where gratitude drives performance better than any methodology.
key_points: Agile methodologies evolved from consultants treating corporate clients like infants with index cards, then pivoted to selling the same snake oil directly to developers
Software productivity is nearly impossible to measure scientifically, which allows fad methodologies to thrive on anecdotes just like fad diets
Google's engineering culture works because launches are the 'least-energy state'—incentives, peer recognition, and gratitude naturally drive the right behaviors without heavy project management
Date-driven development is fundamentally flawed because it ignores human biorhythms, creativity cycles, and the unpredictable nature of software work
A simple priority queue of work items provides all the benefits Agile claims to offer without the bureaucratic overhead
money_quotes: Agile Methodologies are for chumps.
You can't have the same team do the same project twice; a bunch of stuff changes the second time around.
The thing that drives the right behavior at Google, more than anything else, more than all the other things combined, is gratitude.
Google really does understand that you can't rush good cooking, you can't rush babies out, and you can't rush software development.
If a process is potentially good, but 90+% of the time smart and well-intentioned people screw it up, then it's a bad process.
At Google, projects launch because it's the least-energy state for the system.
Bad Agile folks keep their eye on the goal, which hurts innovation.
This impedance mismatch drives great engineers to mediocrity.
posts.key_insight: Great software emerges not from process and methodology, but from creating an environment where launching is the natural outcome—through aligned incentives, autonomy, and genuine care for engineers.

posts.summary: Yegge follows up his controversial 'Good Agile Bad Agile' post by clarifying that Google's approach isn't the only alternative to Agile—most great software developers worldwide simply work hard and ship without any methodology. He argues that Agile is a niche practice amplified by loud marketing and consulting money, not a proven approach. The core of his argument is that Agile is technological superstition: like a chicken that learns to twist while playing piano, teams attribute success to their methodology when hard work is the actual cause. He warns that Agile's viral spread threatens to create workplace discrimination against non-believers, and closes with the revelation that 'Agile Manifesto' has exactly one two-word anagram: 'Egomania Itself.'
key_points: Most successful software companies (Microsoft, Amazon, Blizzard, etc.) don't use Agile—they just work hard and ship great products
Agile's apparent success is survivorship bias: 'if you start with dog shit and add enough chili, you get chili'—hard work produces results regardless of methodology
Superstition is any belief not based on scientific experiment, and our brains naturally conflate coincidence with causation, leading to false beliefs in methodologies
The Agile community's lack of humor and aggressive evangelism are warning signs of a cult-like movement that could harm developers' careers
Static typing preferences (neat-freak vs. slob) parallel methodology preferences—both are personal style choices, not productivity determinants
money_quotes: Most great software developers around the world don't use Agile. They just work hard, they stay lightweight, and they ship great stuff.
You can start with dog shit, and if you add enough chili, you get chili.
Agile works just fine. The problem is much more subtle than that.
If you do a rain dance for enough days in a row, it will eventually work. Guaranteed.
Any time you find a community whose overall Humor Level is rated at 'Homeland Security', it's a pretty good indicator that you don't ever want to have to deal with them.
Whenever you hear Agile people asking around for 'success stories', remind them politely that only looking at the positives is pseudoscience.
As far as I'm concerned, Agile Methodologies are fine for you to use, but if you try to push them on a coworker, it should be treated as an HR violation.
posts.key_insight: Agile methodology is technological superstition—teams attribute their success to the methodology when hard work is the actual cause, just like a chicken that thinks its twisting dance produces food pellets.

posts.summary: Yegge returns after a blogging hiatus to reflect on his one-year blog anniversary and the phenomenon he calls 'Essay Molasses' - the slow, delayed spread of ideas that eventually reach critical mass. He then delivers a rambling critique of Agile methodology, comparing it to religious ceremony and arguing it's only useful for replicating derivative work, not creating anything truly innovative. The post concludes with his core philosophy: continuous self-improvement and becoming so valuable that you can 'call your own shots' rather than being subject to process-driven management.
key_points: Essay Molasses: good ideas encoded in thorough essays spread slowly at first, but their survival depends on that longer format - short takes don't have lasting impact
Agile is fundamentally about ceremony and ritual, which Yegge sees as inherently phony and at odds with getting real work done
Agile methodologies can only predict and manage the delivery of derivative work - you can't stand-up-meeting your way to great art or innovation
The real 'silver keyboard' is continuous learning: every program you write should be the hardest you've ever written
If you become a superstar through relentless self-improvement, employers will alter their business plans to accommodate you
money_quotes: People accuse me of being long-winded, but it's not often that breaking short wind, amusing as it can be, has any real long-term effect.
Most people would rather be right about something trivial, or simply not be heard at all, than be wrong occasionally in the quest to get their ideas out to others.
You can't predict the delivery or quality of great art, unless maybe you consider Thomas Kinkade to be Great Art.
Agile is really all about being able to predict and manage the delivery of stuff that others have delivered. The same old tired websites, the same old inventory-management systems.
I think every program you write should be the hardest you've ever written.
Never stop learning.
If you're a superstar, then your management chain needs you.
posts.key_insight: Long-form writing gives ideas the 'gestation period' they need to survive and spread, and continuous self-improvement makes you immune to process-cargo-cults like Agile.

posts.summary: This allegorical short story depicts a bureaucratic security guard (T.S.) who enforces rules with inflexible rigidity, refusing to help a desperate woman (Anushri) whose husband is critically injured. The guard is revealed to be a Java-based software system, complete with a terrible API and no unit tests. The crowd of passengers eventually solves the problem by collectively stepping out of line, allowing Anushri to be 'next' without breaking any rules. The story satirizes rigid software systems, bureaucratic thinking, and the absurdity of rules without exceptions, while celebrating human creativity in working around inflexible systems.
key_points: Bureaucratic systems accumulate rules for every edge case until they become massive, inflexible, and inhuman
The guard T.S. is literally a Java system with a terrible API (checkCredentials takes a CredentialFactory instead of a simple Passenger object)
No unit tests in production means no way to substitute bypass logic - a critique of untestable, rigid code
Human collaboration and lateral thinking can route around broken systems without technically breaking rules
The system forgets the incident entirely because 'no rules were broken' - it cannot learn or adapt
money_quotes: T.S. needed a special rule for each possible way to break the orderly workings of the line, because otherwise people would argue endlessly. You had to show them that it was written down, a real rule, before they'd listen.
After you got past T.S., you didn't have that warm feeling of being one of the good guys. You felt like you had narrowly escaped being in trouble.
Troublemakers always thought they were special cases.
CredentialFactory? How the hell does anyone get through this line? Why doesn't the API just take a Passenger?
You bastard! Why couldn't they have written you in JavaScript!
There's only one way through this jerk, and it's through this line. And that means we can't be in it.
No rules had been broken. He had performed his duties to the letter. The line was orderly, and the terminal was secure.
posts.key_insight: Rigid, over-engineered systems with terrible APIs and no flexibility will always be routed around by humans who understand that sometimes the rules need to bend for compassion.

posts.summary: Yegge argues that the fundamental problem with software is that we build 'dead' systems requiring constant reboots, rather than 'living' systems that can grow and evolve while running. He identifies key properties of great software—command shells, advice systems, extension languages, plugin architectures, and introspection—all deriving from the principle that systems should never need to reboot. He critiques static type systems as tools for building inflexible 'hardware' rather than adaptable software, and suggests that truly great software exhibits an ineffable 'Quality Without a Name' (QWAN) that emerges from organic growth rather than rigid design.
key_points: The most important principle in software design is that systems should never reboot—if designed this way, they will eventually become systems that can live forever
Great software systems share essential features: command shells, advice/hook systems, extension languages, plugin architectures, and deep introspection capabilities
Static type systems are for building 'hardware'—deterministic, inflexible layers that trade flexibility for performance—while dynamic languages enable building true 'software' that can grow
Most programmers build 'marionettes' (dead, controllable systems) rather than 'real children' (living, evolving software) because living software is scary and harder to manage
Software can possess consciousness on a gradient, and rebooting a sufficiently complex system may eventually become ethically equivalent to killing it
money_quotes: I think most software is crap. Well, that's not quite right. It's fairer to say that I think all software is crap.
Systems should never reboot. If you design a system so that it never needs to reboot, then you will eventually, even if it's by a very roundabout path, arrive at a system that will live forever.
Living software is a little scary. It's no surprise that most programmers prefer writing marionettes instead of real people; marionettes are a lot easier to manage.
There's nothing wrong with static type systems. You just have to realize that when you use them, you're building hardware, not software.
I think of most of the software we build today as being like setting up dominoes. It's Domino Design.
The reason H-M isn't used in the industry, folks, is that it doesn't have any holes.
We will never be able to make real progress in computing and language design in our industry until C syntax is wholly eradicated: a task that could take fifty years.
If you treat your software like a living thing, then eventually it just might become one.
posts.key_insight: The best software systems are 'living' rather than 'dead'—designed to grow and evolve without rebooting—and this single principle drives all other qualities that make software great.

posts.summary: Yegge's friends stage an intervention about his notoriously long blog posts, convincing him to try writing shorter, columnist-style entries of about 800 words per week. He uses this as an opportunity to demonstrate Emacs Lisp programming by building a character-counting function from scratch. The post walks through creating an interactive Emacs command that counts non-whitespace characters, explaining concepts like save-excursion, let bindings, and the while loop. Despite promising brevity, the post about writing shorter posts ends up being nearly double his target length, a self-aware irony Yegge acknowledges at the end.
key_points: Friends convinced him his posts are roughly 2400 times too long, leading to an experiment in brevity
Emacs's power comes from commands being scripted versions of manual operations - learning the editor teaches you to program it
The save-excursion macro is fundamental to Emacs programming, preserving cursor position during operations
Lisp syntax is simple once you understand it's just (function arg arg...) all the way down, nesting like XML without the verbosity
Eclipse and Visual Studio's plugin systems are so heavyweight that developers never customize their tools
money_quotes: I can be like Paul Graham, and sit around in my underwear writing articles about how I get to sit around in my underwear writing articles about stuff, on account of having written everything in Lisp. Except I'll still have to wear pants.
As you learn the editor commands, you're also learning how to program Emacs, because you can use all those commands in your elisp code.
Is it any wonder that developers never customize their tools?
It's just (function arg arg...). If arg is a call to a function, then you parenthesize it, and it all nests nicely, sorta like XML but without all the yelling.
The extra parens date back to 1955, and they get real grumpy if you mention them, so let's not.
That's what they used to say about Emacs, but then hardware got faster so they needed a new elephant.
posts.key_insight: Emacs's extensibility model, where editor commands and programming primitives are unified, creates a uniquely powerful environment that modern IDEs with their heavyweight plugin systems have failed to replicate.

posts.summary: Yegge predicts the imminent arrival of the 'Next Big Language' (NBL) and outlines the characteristics it must have to achieve mass adoption. He argues that language success requires corporate backing, C-like syntax (despite its flaws), dynamic typing with optional static types, strong performance, excellent tooling, and a comprehensive feature set. The post acknowledges that these requirements optimize for popularity rather than elegance, noting that the bar for 'not sucking' has become remarkably high. Yegge claims to know what NBL is but refuses to name it, instead providing enough hints for readers to deduce it themselves.
key_points: Most new programming languages fail because they lack corporate backing and don't meet the practical requirements for mass adoption
C-like syntax is mandatory for popularity despite being overly complicated and making code-processing tools difficult to write
Dynamic typing with optional static types is superior to static typing with bolted-on dynamism - the latter is 'premature optimization'
The minimum feature set for a modern language includes closures, iterators, list comprehensions, first-class parser support, and cross-platform GUI
Performance comparable to Java is essential, which requires clever solutions around eval() and other dynamic features
money_quotes: If you want to spare yourself a lot of angst in deciding which programming language to use, then I recommend this simple rule: Go ugly early.
Having a deep understanding of compilers is what separates the wheat from the chaff.
We have yet to see a language that is (a) popular and (b) doesn't suck. Do you disagree? If so, I don't mind. It just means your standards aren't as high as mine.
Adding in optional static types is the ideal solution... static + optional dynamic features is premature optimization, plain and simple: the root of all evil.
One of the biggest reasons people haven't adopted Ruby or Python is the lack of IDE support.
Ruby doesn't actually need automated refactoring tools in the way Java does; it's like refusing to switch to an electric car because there's no place to put the gasoline.
The features I've outlined are basically the minimal set of requirements for not sucking.
Nobody ever accuses programmers of being wise.
posts.key_insight: Language adoption is determined not by elegance or technical superiority, but by meeting a high bar of practical requirements including familiar syntax, corporate backing, strong tooling, and comprehensive features - optimizing for programmer expectations rather than ideals.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge shares diary entries from his first weeks as a Google employee ('Noogler') in 2005, describing the culture shock of abundant free food, the quirky perks like ball pits and massage chairs, and the exodus of Microsoft employees to Google's Kirkland office. The post humorously documents mishaps like losing his badge in a ball pit and escaping a boring tech talk, while painting a picture of Google as an engineering paradise where former Microsoft employees are thrilled to 'actually be writing code' again. The underlying message is that Google had become the gravitational center of tech talent, with an 'event horizon' that inevitably pulls in anyone who investigates it seriously.
key_points: Google's recruiting power had reached the point where investigating why people choose Google inevitably leads to applying there yourself

The Kirkland office was populated largely by ex-Microsoft employees who shared similar stories: cool projects that never launched, teams that hated each other, and bureaucratic weight crushing progress
Former Microsoft employees universally expressed relief at Google, saying they were 'actually writing code' again
The free food culture was so overwhelming that Yegge nearly got sick on his first day, comparing it to Willy Wonka's factory
Despite the perks and humor, Yegge jokes that Google is fattening employees up like the witch in Hansel and Gretel
money_quotes: Google has a sort of event horizon, beyond which you're inevitably sucked in.
Anyone at another company who looks hard enough into why people choose Google will ultimately apply to Google, if they have the courage.
Project was cool, never launched it, everyone hates each other.
If you ask anyone: 'are you glad you came to Google?' They always brighten up instantly and say: 'oh, hell yeah! I'm actually writing code!'
Nobody just gives you a bunch of free food for as long as you want without killing you and eating you.
They didn't prepare me adequately for the experience of being surrounded by yummy free food. I had approximately the same reaction as the kids when they first got to Willy Wonka's.
I think I'm the only person who's necessitated consulting the rules to see whether it counts if you serve, then score on yourself without the ball touching the other player's men.
posts.key_insight: Google's combination of engineering autonomy, abundant perks, and freedom from bureaucracy created a talent black hole that was systematically draining other tech companies of their best engineers.

posts.summary: Yegge uses an elaborate fantasy tavern allegory to describe a toxic workplace situation, likely at a major tech company. The narrator and his friend Rauser are master builders who both fell under 'spells' cast by 'evil wizards' - manipulative political operators who exploit hardworking engineers. Rauser's story describes building a palace on a swamp under impossible deadlines, with constant schedule pressure, rumor campaigns, and the bizarre metaphor of being forced to eat endless marshmallows (busywork/dysfunction). The core message is that organizations of well-meaning 'sheep' are vulnerable to being devoured by political 'wolves' who operate invisibly until it's too late.
key_points: Organizations filled with good-intentioned people are vulnerable to manipulation by a single skilled political operator who can go unrecognized until everyone is 'eaten'
The 'boiling frog' phenomenon applies to workplace dysfunction - problems accumulate gradually until workers can't recognize how bad things have gotten
Wolves/wizards specialize in being invisible to sheep - they use fear, false hope, and manufactured crises to prevent workers from fighting back
When sheep (workers) are finally devoured publicly, the spell breaks, but by then enormous damage has been done
The solution isn't simply removing wolves - a clumsy wolf will be replaced by a subtler one until you can't distinguish predators from prey
money_quotes: I was entrapped by a spell cast by an evil wizard, old friend, that's just me doing my best to explain something entirely outside my realm of experience.
You've heard about boiling frogs, of course, and I tell you: even if it's not true, that wives tale about frog-boiling has the ring of truth because things like it happen all the time.
I was feeling guilty all the time, but I couldn't walk away.
I was being eaten alive on my mansion project, and yet I was utterly oblivious to anything but the need to survive.

If you set up any organization of saintly do-gooders, with no politics anywhere in sight, then the presence of a single master politician can go unrecognized for long enough for every single sheep to be eaten and replaced with a wolf.
The wolf didn't huff, or puff, or blow my house down. It was the wolf from Red Riding Hood, the fabled wolf in sheep's clothing, and its craft was far beyond mine, since I have none.
Sheep can kick surprisingly hard, as many a wild wolf has found. They simply lack the will to do it.
posts.key_insight: Political operators in tech companies act like wolves among sheep - they exploit engineers' good faith and lack of political awareness to create toxic, unsustainable work environments while remaining invisible until catastrophic damage is done.

posts.summary: Yegge argues that compiler construction is the second most important computer science course because it integrates nearly everything else you learn in CS and equips you to solve real programming problems that constantly arise. He confesses to failing compilers the first time and only later understanding why they matter: compilers are everywhere, from syntax highlighting to code refactoring to DSLs. The post skewers programmers who avoid compilers, mocks the type-checking academic camp, and insists that writing your own compiler—despite becoming 'a lifetime of work'—is essential to truly understanding how computers work. Without this knowledge, you're condemned to the 'programmer B-list,' building bloated systems while waiting for someone else to fix your tools.
key_points: If you don't know how compilers work, you don't know how computers work—and most programmers (even Ph.D.s) can't explain compiler basics
Compilation problems are everywhere: syntax highlighting, doc extraction, code refactoring, DSLs, and processing any structured symbol stream
The three compiler camps (parsing, type checking, code generation/optimization) are largely isolated from each other and from practical programming
Static typing zealots have mistaken their limited domain for the boundary of all useful computation, ignoring probabilistic approaches
Writing a compiler yourself is the only way to truly learn a language and escape dependence on tools others build for you
money_quotes: If you don't know how compilers work, then you don't know how computers work.
You can't fully understand how compilers work without knowing machine architecture, because compilers emit machine code.
If you don't take compilers then you run the risk of forever being on the programmer B-list.
Large Systems Suck. This rule is 100% transitive. If you build one, you suck.
Writing a compiler creates a living thing. I didn't realize this going into it. I wasn't asking for a baby.
Someday they will realize that strict determinism has always, always failed, in every dimensionality-cursed domain to which it's ever been applied, and it's always replaced by probabilistic methods.
You'll be able to jump in and help fix all those problems with your favorite language. Don't even try to tell me your favorite language doesn't have problems.
posts.key_insight: Compiler knowledge is the gateway from being a tool user to being a tool maker—without it, you'll forever depend on others to solve the parsing and transformation problems that pervade all of programming.

posts.summary: Yegge recounts his unexpectedly viral Foo Camp talk about porting Ruby on Rails to JavaScript running on the JVM via Mozilla Rhino. He explains Google's pragmatic language policy (C++, Java, Python, JavaScript only) and why this constraint, combined with his project's JVM requirements, led him to choose Rhino over Jython. The post details how they extended JavaScript with Ruby/Python-like methods and began a 20% project to add JavaScript 1.7 features to Rhino. He emphasizes that Google's clean codebase and engineering discipline enable this kind of experimentation within well-defined boundaries.
key_points: Google limits production languages to C++, Java, Python, and JavaScript to build language expertise, reduce interoperability complexity, and enable easy team transfers
Rhino (server-side JavaScript on JVM) was chosen over Jython because Jython stagnated since 2001 while Rhino has momentum, a real spec, and Sun's endorsement in Java 6
They extended JavaScript with Ruby and Python built-in methods by leveraging Rhino's ability to define non-enumerable properties
The Rails port covers ActionView, ActionController, and Railties, done manually with Emacs keyboard macros rather than code generators
Google's engineering culture requires design documents for anything over a week of work, resulting in comprehensive internal documentation for every significant piece of code
money_quotes: I am privileged to work at Google. It's a privilege. It's something that I have no expectations about.
I'm nobody at Google. I'm a very comfy, very small fish in a sea full of large, toothy sea creatures.
The thing is, Google's decision to limit the number of languages allowed for production work is actually pretty smart.
Programming languages have a core of standard functionality that everyone knows, followed by a long tail of murky semantics that become increasingly difficult to figure out.
I've never seen anything like it before, to be honest. I don't think you can get that kind of software-engineering discipline without doing it right from the start.
Ports are kinda boring when you get right down to it.
Rails is its own most succinct implementation, with very few exceptions.
posts.key_insight: Smart language constraints at scale aren't limiting—they enable experimentation by building deep expertise and reducing organizational friction.

posts.summary: Yegge recounts the chaotic behind-the-scenes story of how he came to give an OSCON keynote talk about marketing and branding. The piece weaves together his attendance at Foo Camp, where he witnessed OSI's futile attempt to protect the 'Open Source' trademark, with his last-minute preparation for a keynote he didn't expect anyone to attend. He discovers too late that he's the closing keynote speaker for 3,000 people, gets shepherded through a surreal backstage experience by a judgmental handler, and faces technical difficulties right as his talk begins. The post ends on a cliffhanger, promising the actual talk content in a follow-up.
key_points: The OSI's attempt to protect 'Open Source' through federation and angry letters is doomed because the term was never trademarked and has no clear definition, unlike Stallman's 'Free Software'
Marketing isn't about banner ads—it's about what your friends are saying, as illustrated by the mountain bike seat that failed because pros mocked it
Yegge chose to talk about marketing despite knowing almost nothing about it, reasoning he'd read one marketing book which was one more than most programmers
The behind-the-scenes reality of keynote speaking involves sneaky technicians, lost dongles, and crawling to the front row to avoid blocking the projector
money_quotes: Marketing. It's not the banner ads. It's what your friends are saying.
If it's not trademarked, and you can't protect it legally, and it's just two vanilla words concatenated, then you're screwed.
Me? I was all for changing the name. What the hell does Open Source mean, anyway?
I decided, then and there, with me not knowing Jack Squat about marketing, to do my OSCON presentation on marketing.
He walks past me, stops, looks me up and down with the exact same expression the restaurant waiters in Paris used on me.
At Foo Camp when someone's giving a talk that sucks, it's kind of hard to escape politely.
posts.key_insight: The open source movement's branding problem stems from failing to clearly define and legally protect what 'Open Source' actually means, leaving the term vulnerable to corporate co-optation.

posts.summary: This satirical piece presents fake tech news stories that skewer various targets in the software industry. Yegge lampoons Java's verbosity and Eclipse's notorious startup time, takes shots at Ruby on Rails' scaling problems, mocks Microsoft's legacy code, and delivers a pointed commentary on the absurdity of using recursion questions in technical interviews. The piece is structured as a mock newsletter with increasingly absurd stories, culminating in juvenile humor about a vacuum cleaner.
key_points: Java's design pattern culture creates meaningless complexity - combining pattern names produces absurd constructs like 'FactoryObserverBridgeChainOfCommandSingletonProxy'
Eclipse IDE was notorious for extremely slow startup times and unresponsiveness
Ruby on Rails faced legitimate scaling criticism in 2007, satirized here with URLs that could delete databases
The recursion vs iteration equivalence is humorously 'disproven' by noting recursion's real purpose: filtering interview candidates
Microsoft's legacy codebase issues are mocked by suggesting Vista still runs code from Bill Gates' childhood
money_quotes: Java programmers understand an average of seven fewer Computer Science concepts per hour spent with Java each day
The last thing anyone heard him say aloud was the cryptic comment: 'I see the splash screen is appropriately blue.'
The little security system that Bill wrote at age 12 and hacked into a year later is still in production today, in Windows Vista
Some of our users are just plain wankers
There is in fact one thing you can do using recursion that you can't do using iteration, namely: 'You can use it to weed out stupid fugging interview candidates.'
posts.key_insight: Tech industry sacred cows - from Java's enterprise patterns to algorithm-focused interviews - deserve relentless mockery because they've become rituals disconnected from actual software quality.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge provides brutally honest advice on writing technical resumes, drawing from his experience screening over 5,000 resumes and interviewing 1,200+ candidates. He argues that resumes should be stripped of personality, kept in plain text, and focused entirely on demonstrable skills. The post systematically dismantles common resume practices: weasel words that hide lack of contribution, wank words that inflate importance, certifications that signal weakness, and lies that always get caught. He emphasizes that resume screening is pure pattern matching—nobody cares about you as a person yet, they just want to assess your skills quickly.
key_points: Resume screening is pattern matching, not personality assessment—delete all personal information, hobbies, and cover letters unless directly relevant to programming
Use plain ASCII text only; any fancy formatting, Unicode, or Word-specific characters will be mangled into unreadable garbage
Avoid 'weasel words' like participated, proposed, and analyzed that hide whether you actually accomplished anything; use 'productivity words' like coded, implemented, and launched
'Wank words' like certified, architect, methodologist, and consultant are active red flags that signal you don't do real work
Never lie or exaggerate on technical skills—interviewers will always find the one thing you claimed to know and drill into it
money_quotes: Resume screening is just pattern matching. People are trying to figure out if you have the skills they're looking for.
"Utilize" has been scientifically demonstrated to be used only by stupid people, so if you use it you could easily be mistaken for one.
I can say truthfully that I participated in the Gulf War. I even received a medal for it. The actual form of my participation involved watching it on CNN.
Certification is for the weak. It's something that flags you as a technician when you really want to be an engineer.
In the ancient and occult secret rosetta-stone decoder language of technical resume screeners everywhere, "eager to learn" means "unskilled labor".
If you lie on a tech resume, you'll get caught. Of course one of the interviewers is going to be a passionate closet Forth user.
"Motivated" is another resume-screening synonym for "desperate". Don't say you're motivated. It's like wearing a suit to the interview.
posts.key_insight: Technical resumes should be stripped down to verifiable facts about what you actually built and shipped, because everything else—personality, certifications, buzzwords, and exaggerations—actively works against you in the pattern-matching game of resume screening.

posts.summary: Yegge writes a casual status update after a minor scare where an automated system disabled his Google account, leading Reddit to speculate he was being fired.
