He announces he's working on a book about Mozilla Rhino with Norris Boyd, building a JavaScript interpreter in Emacs-Lisp to create a world-class JavaScript IDE and allow writing Emacs extensions in JavaScript. He also weighs in on the ECMAScript 4 debate, obliquely criticizing Microsoft's opposition, and reaffirms his love for working at Google.
key_points: An automated system accidentally disabled his Google account, causing brief panic and Reddit speculation about his firing
He's co-writing a book on Mozilla Rhino with Norris Boyd, the primary author of Rhino
He's building a full JavaScript interpreter in Emacs-Lisp to create a JavaScript IDE for Emacs and allow writing Emacs extensions in JS instead of elisp
He criticizes 'Uncle Mike' (Microsoft) for opposing ECMAScript 4 and encourages people to switch to Firefox
He's experimenting with limiting blog posts to ~1000 words to publish more frequently
money_quotes: I had an exciting morning of not getting fired today.
This automated system had apparently just watched the movie Brazil, and thought it would be fun to send me off to Information Retrieval.
I wish I could get back to High School Time, where even a single hour-long class can seem like eternity, and 4 years is effectively infinite.
JavaScript turns out to be a better language, now that I know them both in excruciating detail.
Emacs is a great environment that needs a better extension language.
That's the elevator pitch: puts people to sleep in 20 seconds, guaranteed.
I still use Emacs to an extent that could justifiably be described as 'unhealthy'.
Like my friend Dominic says, 'it feels like I've won the lottery every day.'
posts.key_insight: JavaScript is poised to become a Next Big Language, and its elegance makes it a better choice than Emacs-Lisp for extending Emacs despite the unconventional implementation path.

posts.summary: Yegge argues that code base size is the worst enemy of software development, a minority view he developed after creating a 500,000-line Java game that became unmanageable for a single developer. He contends that Java's static type system and lack of compression facilities (macros, lambdas, declarative structures) forces inevitable bloat, while industry 'solutions' like IDEs and design patterns actually make code bases larger rather than smaller. After years of evaluation, he concludes that switching to a more expressive JVM language like JavaScript (Rhino/ES4), Ruby, or Python could compress a well-engineered Java codebase by 50-75%. He predicts that while Java programmers remain in denial about the problem, alternative JVM languages will eventually become compelling replacements.
key_points: Code base size/bloat is the worst thing that can happen to a project, team, or company - a minority view that only comes from painful personal experience with massive codebases
Java's static type system lacks compression facilities, making bloat inevitable - it's like Tetris where pieces can never fill gaps, only pile up endlessly
Design patterns and refactoring, while organizing code better, actually make codebases larger rather than smaller
IDEs create a circular problem: they encourage static languages that require IDEs, while dynamic features that could compress code defeat IDE capabilities
The solution is switching to more expressive JVM languages (JavaScript/Rhino, JRuby, Jython) that could compress a 500k-line Java codebase to 125k-150k lines
money_quotes: I believe that code weight wrecks projects and companies, that it forces rewrites after a certain size, and that smart teams will do everything in their power to keep their code base from becoming a mountain.
Java is like a variant of the game of Tetris in which none of the pieces can fill gaps created by the other pieces, so all you can do is pile them up endlessly.
Design Patterns was a mid-1990s book that provided twenty-three fancy new boxes for organizing your closet, plus an extensibility mechanism for defining new types of boxes.
If you begin with the assumption that you need to shrink your code base, you will eventually be forced to conclude that you cannot continue to use Java.
An 'X programmer', for any value of X, is a weak player. You have to cross-train to be a decent athlete these days.
Refactoring is like cleaning your closet without being allowed to throw anything away.
People who don't habitually spit will expectorate up to thirty feet, like zoo camels, in order to bespittle you if you even suggest the possibility of using a Lisp or Scheme dialect at your company.
posts.tone: opinionated, conversational, self-deprecating humor mixed with genuine frustration
posts.key_insight: The worst thing that can happen to a codebase is size, and Java's fundamental character makes bloat inevitable - the only solution is switching to more expressive languages that provide real compression facilities.

posts.summary: Yegge defends his notoriously long blog posts against critics who want shorter entries. He argues that his posts are intentionally essay-length (around 10 pages) because longer content has better 'survival characteristics' - greater reach and lasting impact. He presents a 'brain-cache theory' suggesting the ideal essay length should fill a reader's mental short-term cache and force ideas to be committed to long-term memory. This requires roughly one 'sitting' of 20-50 minutes, which is why his posts are calibrated to be exhausting but not crushing.
key_points: Blog length and popularity have a causative relationship - bigger entries create bigger splashes, though there's an upper limit before readers drop off
The ideal essay length is measured in duration (one sitting of ~50 minutes max), not word count, because the goal is to fill the reader's mental cache
Essays aim to 'imprint' readers with ideas by forcing content from short-term memory into long-term storage through sustained engagement
Different readers will remember different parts of an essay due to randomness in how our brains discard information, so good essays say the same thing multiple ways
Blogs are the best available 'turn-key essay publishing system' even though readers arrive expecting cat pictures instead of 10-page arguments
money_quotes: I'm doing it on purpose.
It's as if you're all in a pond, and I'm throwing a rock into it. Bigger rock, bigger splash.
If you want to make a lasting impression, then you need to fill up the reader's second-level cache and start blowing pages out into their long-term memory.
It's one of the amazing miracles of the internet: write-only people. They can't read but they somehow find a way to write.
The right length for an essay, I believe, is exactly 'one sitting': no more, no less.
If I throw in a sufficiently large rock, it'll crush you, which for most of you is an undesirable outcome.
My blogs may or may not be any good, but they're widely read. So are they really too long?
By getting you to disagree with it, I've left my mark. At least you'll remember the idea now.
posts.key_insight: Long-form writing succeeds not despite reader fatigue but because of it - exhausting the reader's mental short-term cache forces ideas into permanent memory.

posts.summary: This post is a practical primer on Emacs Lisp aimed at programmers who know C, Java, or JavaScript but want to customize Emacs. Rather than teaching the 'Tao of Lisp' with philosophical abstractions, Yegge provides a cookbook-style reference mapping familiar programming constructs to their Elisp equivalents. The guide covers lexical elements, operators, control flow, functions, error handling, and even a basic object system using defstruct. Yegge acknowledges Elisp's quirks while arguing it's worth learning for the power it gives you over your editor.
key_points: Elisp is best learned by mapping familiar constructs from C/Java/JavaScript rather than through abstract Lisp philosophy
The Common Lisp (cl) package provides essential features like loop macros, increment operators, and defstruct for pseudo-OOP
Emacs Lisp uses dynamic scoping, which is 'generally considered evil' but occasionally useful for modifying caller stack variables
The catch/throw mechanism handles both early returns and break/continue semantics, while condition-case handles true errors
Buffers can be treated as class instances using buffer-local variables as encapsulated instance data
money_quotes: Most Lisp introductions try to give you the 'Tao of Lisp', complete with incense-burning, chanting, yoga and all that stuff. What I really wanted in the beginning was a simple cookbook for doing my 'normal' stuff in Lisp.
Dynamic scoping is generally considered a bad design bordering on evil, but it can occasionally come in handy.
As a language it's not that great, and everyone wishes it were Common Lisp or Scheme or some other reasonable Lisp dialect. Some people even wish it weren't Lisp at all, if you can believe that!
It's sometimes a good idea to slap an ignore-errors around bits of elisp code in your startup file that may not always work, so you can still at least start your Emacs up if the code is failing.
Java may suck at declaring constructors, but Emacs Lisp makes up for it by sucking at setting fields.
In Elisp programming it can often be useful to think of buffers as instances of your own classes.
A little Elisp goes a long way.
posts.key_insight: Learning Elisp doesn't require embracing Lisp philosophy—you can be productive by simply mapping familiar imperative programming patterns to their Lisp equivalents.

posts.summary: Yegge argues that programmer maturity can be measured by their 'compression tolerance' - the ability to read and write dense code without excessive comments or metadata. He compares junior programmers' verbose commenting style to a 2-year-old's temporal narratives, both being attempts to make sense of complexity through storytelling. The post extends this metaphor to static typing, arguing that over-reliance on type systems and metadata modeling is another form of n00b behavior. He concludes that experienced programmers should resist the urge to model everything and instead focus on getting code written and making forward progress.
key_points: Programmer maturity correlates with tolerance for code compression - n00bs need verbose comments while veterans prefer dense, self-documenting code
Static types are just metadata/comments targeted at programmers and compilers, and can be overused just like comments
Java's success partly comes from enabling 'metadata addiction' - letting programmers spend time modeling instead of shipping
The more 'sound' a type system tries to be, the more often it's 'wrong' when it can't express what you actually need
When in doubt, don't model it - excessive metadata has real costs in maintenance, flexibility, and development speed
money_quotes: If you're a n00b, you'll look at experienced code and say it's impenetrable, undisciplined crap written by someone who never learned the essentials of modern software engineering.

Static types tell a story about the computation, presumably to help both reader groups understand the intent of the program. But the static types can be thrown away at runtime, because in the end they're just stylized comments.
Perl, Python and Ruby fail to attract many Java and C++ programmers because, well, they force you to get stuff done.
Haskell, OCaml and their ilk are part of a 45-year-old static-typing movement within academia to try to force people to model everything. Programmers hate that.
Java has been overrun by metadata-addicted n00bs.
When in doubt, don't model it. Just get the code written, make forward progress.
A sufficiently large model can outweigh its own benefits. Even an IDE can't make things go faster when you have ten thousand classes in your system.
posts.tone: provocative, irreverent, and deliberately contrarian with characteristic Yegge humor and self-deprecation
posts.key_insight: Excessive commenting, static typing, and metadata modeling are all manifestations of the same insecurity - the n00b's need to tell themselves stories about code rather than just writing it.

posts.summary: Yegge provides a comprehensive guide to interviewing at Google, acknowledging upfront that his advice will upset people who don't know the topics he recommends studying. He explains the concept of the 'Interview Anti-Loop' - the reality that every employee has a set of coworkers who wouldn't have hired them, making interviews inherently random. The post covers both mental preparation (stay humble, don't question the interviewer's questions) and technical preparation (algorithms, data structures, graphs, Big-O, operating systems). He emphasizes that Google's high false-negative rate means rejection doesn't reflect your actual abilities, and encourages candidates to reapply after 6-12 months if they don't succeed initially.
key_points: Every employee has an 'Interview Anti-Loop' - a set of coworkers who would not have hired them, making interviews inherently flawed and somewhat random
Google intentionally has a high false-negative rate, preferring to reject qualified candidates over hiring unqualified ones
You cannot tell interviewers how to interview - they believe they're good at it after about one year and never change their methods
Technical preparation should focus on algorithms, data structures (especially graphs and hashtables), Big-O complexity, and concurrency fundamentals
Warm up before interviews like an athlete: study for weeks beforehand and practice intensely the morning of the interview
money_quotes: Everyone who applied had the same reasons for not applying as you do. And everyone here says: 'GOSH, I SURE AM HAPPY I CAME HERE!'
That's almost always what happens when you get an offer from a tech company. You just happened to squeak by.
A and B would probably not even hire each other, given a chance, but they both happened to go through interviewer C.
Hashtables are arguably the single most important data structure known to mankind.
Whenever someone gives you a problem, think graphs. They are the most fundamental and flexible way of representing any kind of a relationship.
The best way to appear arrogant is to question the validity of the interviewer's question – it really ticks them off.
Real-world work makes you rusty.
posts.key_insight: Tech interviews are inherently flawed and random due to interviewer bias and the 'Anti-Loop' phenomenon, so rejection doesn't reflect your abilities - prepare thoroughly, stay humble, and try again if you fail.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge reviews four console games he's played over the past year: Portal, Mario Galaxy, Zelda: Twilight Princess, and Uncharted: Drake's Fortune. He praises Portal's innovative gameplay and humor, calls Mario Galaxy possibly the best Mario since the original, admires Twilight Princess's emotional storytelling, and gushes over Uncharted's stunning visuals on his new PS3 and plasma TV. The post also includes his thoughts on console hardware preferences, particularly his enthusiasm for the Wii's innovative controls and his recent HD setup upgrade. Throughout, he maintains his characteristic conversational style while offering genuine recommendations for fellow gamers who share his taste in adventure and RPG titles.
key_points: Portal is a flawless short masterpiece with innovative gameplay and surprisingly funny writing
Mario Galaxy arguably surpasses all previous Mario titles with exceptional music, atmosphere, and level design
Twilight Princess features an emotionally powerful story arc, particularly the 'Midna's Desperate Hour' sequence
Uncharted: Drake's Fortune showcases the PS3's visual potential with graphics that surpass even Blu-Ray movies
The Wii's motion controls make traditional controllers feel like 'Atari Joysticks' from the 1980s
money_quotes: Going back to the XBox 360 or Playstation controllers feels totally rinky-dink, like I've traveled back to the 1980s and I'm using an Atari Joystick.
Mario Galaxy is arguably the best Mario title since the original Super Mario Bros. arcade game.
I've never played a game before that made it feel like all five senses were in play the way this game does.
Nothing, nothing I've seen so far on my new screen, not Blu-Ray discs, not HD channels, not Oblivion on the XBox 360, nothing can compare to the visual feast provided by Drake's Fortune.
I pretty much bought my XBox 360 for Oblivion... It didn't matter, though, since Oblivion was good enough to warrant the purchase of an XBox 360.
The game is sumptuous, no better word for it.
posts.key_insight: Great games justify entire console purchases, and the best ones create such immersive experiences that they engage all your senses and leave lasting emotional memories.

posts.summary: Yegge announces js2-mode, a new JavaScript editing mode for Emacs that includes a full recursive-descent parser ported from Mozilla Rhino. The mode provides accurate syntax highlighting, async parsing, and bounce-style indentation. He details his failed attempt at incremental parsing and the surprisingly difficult problem of indentation, which he discovered is 'almost totally orthogonal to parsing.' The project is part of a larger effort to enable writing Emacs extensions in JavaScript instead of Emacs-Lisp.
key_points: Built a 10,000-line elisp mode with a full JavaScript parser ported from Mozilla Rhino for accurate syntax highlighting
Incremental parsing proved too complex; switched to asynchronous parsing after weeks of failed effort
Indentation is shockingly harder than parsing - cc-mode has 27,000 lines of lisp just for indentation
Adopted a 'bounce indenter' from Karl Landström's javascript.el that cycles through likely indentation points on TAB
The mode includes syntax error highlighting, strict warnings, code folding, and jsdoc support
money_quotes: I can't recognize my own incompetence
Brendan is an amazingly polite guy, so at the time I didn't realize this was a code-phrase for: 'Let me know when you give up on it, loser.'
27k lines of Lisp code? (Meaning it would be, like, five times that much Java?)
Amazingly, surprisingly, counterintuitively, the indentation problem is almost totally orthogonal to parsing and syntax validation.
Let's face it: you could be a total lunatic, and Emacs has to make you happy.
I'm declaring war on people too lazy to learn to type.
If you spend even 30 seconds on their site it becomes apparent that 'commercial' means 'better'.
posts.key_insight: Building a full parser for syntax highlighting is straightforward compared to the nightmare of indentation, which requires handling an explosion of edge cases that parsers deliberately ignore.

posts.summary: Yegge documents his switch from Windows to Mac OS X, primarily motivated by better font rendering as his eyesight declines with age. He then embarks on a two-day quest to implement focus-follows-mouse (specifically 'autofocus' without autoraise), a feature he's used for 20 years on Unix systems. After diving deep into Apple's Accessibility APIs and Carbon framework, he discovers a fundamental limitation: modifier keys (Control, Alt, Command) are silently dropped when posting keyboard events to background applications. This architectural decision, rooted in Mac's menu-bar-at-top design philosophy, makes true autofocus effectively impossible without OS-level changes that Apple has no motivation to make.
key_points: Focus-follows-mouse has two distinct flavors: autofocus (window gets keyboard focus without coming to front) and autoraise (window comes to front automatically) - most Mac users conflate these
Mac's architectural assumption that the foreground app owns the menu bar means background apps may have incomplete menu structures, making keyboard input potentially crash-inducing
Apple's Accessibility APIs allow posting keyboard events to background apps, but inexplicably drop modifier keys - a limitation that's persisted for at least six years despite developer complaints
The government-mandated Accessibility APIs represent a crack in Apple's 'our way or no way' philosophy, forcing them to expose limited programmatic control over running applications
The cost of not supporting autofocus is losing programmer adoption - users who could become Mac developers but won't switch due to this single missing feature
money_quotes: I'm getting old and the fonts look nicer. Pretty stupid reason, isn't it? I thought so too. But getting old kinda sneaks up on you.
Many programmers feel that autofocus is delicate butterfly and autoraise is a big, stinky buffalo.
Programmers are really, really lazy, and they like to minimize motion. They'd use feeder tubes if the Health Department would let them.
It's amazing how so many people choose to rationalize stuff they're forced to live with. Why not just admit it sucks? Sometimes stuff sucks!
Apple OS engineers don't want you to do stuff that they don't want you to do, and they specifically define 'stuff they don't want you to do' as 'stuff they don't think you want to do.'
It's that whole 'we designed it the right way for everyone' mentality that turns off so many would-be Mac users.
They're all just plumbing for Emacs, anyway. And now my plumbing has nicer fonts.
posts.key_insight: Apple's 'we know best' design philosophy, while producing elegant experiences for most users, creates architectural assumptions so deeply baked into the OS that accommodating power users with different workflows becomes nearly impossible.

posts.summary: Yegge argues that XEmacs, despite its historical importance in pushing Emacs forward with GUI features and innovation, should be retired in favor of GNU Emacs. He presents evidence that XEmacs suffers from chronic instability, poor performance, more bugs, and API incompatibilities that fragment the developer community. The broader argument is that Emacs itself faces existential threats from browsers like Firefox, which combine extensibility with modern rendering capabilities. To compete, the Emacs community must unify behind GNU Emacs and focus on critical improvements: better rendering, multi-language VM support, and concurrency.
key_points: XEmacs historically drove Emacs innovation but now lags behind GNU Emacs in stability, performance, and features
API incompatibilities between XEmacs and GNU Emacs waste developer effort and fragment the community
Emacs is uniquely 'self-hosting' - improvements to the environment compound because you're enhancing the tools you use to make enhancements
The real threat to Emacs isn't IDEs but browsers, which combine extensibility with modern rendering and could eventually eclipse Emacs
Emacs needs three major evolutions to survive: better rendering engine, multi-language VM support, and concurrency
money_quotes: XEmacs has never been a particularly good tool for serious Emacs users because even though it's written in C, it crashes like a mature C++ application.
Emacs is self-hosting: writing things in it makes the environment itself more powerful. It's a feedback loop: a recursive, self-reinforcing, multiplicative effect.

Writing an Eclipse or IntelliJ plugin is a monumental effort, so almost nobody does it. This means there's no community of building and customizing your own tools, which has long been the hallmark of great programmers.
If Emacs can't find a way to evolve into (or merge with) Firefox, then Firefox or some other extensible browser is going to eclipse Emacs.

I suspect that in self-hosted environments, you can eventually cross a threshold where your performance gains from features implemented in the hosted environment outpace the gains from features in the substrate.
Tabs don't scale. Tabbed browsing was revolutionary in the same way adding more tellers to a bank was revolutionary: it's, like, 4x better.
Dynamic typing wins again, as it always will.
posts.key_insight: Software communities must recognize when competition has served its purpose and unification becomes necessary for survival against external threats.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge argues that dynamic languages are poised for a performance renaissance thanks to advanced JIT compilation techniques like polymorphic inline caching and trace trees. He contends that static type systems have reached their limits and increasingly get in the way of large-scale programming, while runtime optimization can achieve better results than static compilation. The talk traces how the AI winter killed dynamic language research, but the Ajax revolution has reignited interest in optimizing JavaScript and other dynamic languages. Despite these technical advances, Yegge pessimistically concludes that language popularity rankings will remain frozen due to programmer inertia and organizational politics.
key_points: Runtime information enables far better optimization than static compilation - the JIT can inline polymorphic methods, predict types, and undo/redo optimizations based on actual execution patterns
Static type systems have failed to deliver on their promise - they add complexity without making languages more expressive, and generic type errors are often incomprehensible
Language adoption is 80% politics and 20% technology - engineers will literally scream at you for suggesting non-mainstream languages, and switching languages is career-threateningly risky
Tools for dynamic languages can achieve feature parity with static IDEs using data-flow analysis and runtime information - the claim that 'no type tags means no information' is false
Small systems are easier to optimize globally than large systems - Geoworks lost to Microsoft despite hand-optimized assembly because they couldn't see how their 15 million line system worked anymore
money_quotes: So javac, the Java compiler: what does it do? Well, it generates bytecode, does some optimizations presumably, and maybe tells you some errors. And then you ship it off to the JVM. And what happens to that bytecode? First thing that happens is they build a tree out of it... The next thing that happens in the JVM is the JIT undoes all the optimizations! Why? So it can do better ones because it has runtime information.
The languages we have today, sorted by popularity at this instant, are probably going to stay about that popular for the next ten years. Sad, isn't it? Very, very sad. But that's the way it is.

You've never been in a company where there's an engineer with a Computer Science degree and ten years of experience, an architect, who's in your face screaming at you, with spittle flying on you, because you suggested using D. Or Haskell. Or Lisp.
Small systems are not only easier to optimize, they're possible to optimize. And I mean globally optimize.
I realized early in my career that I would actually rather have a runtime error than a compile error. Because the debugger gives you this ability to start poking and prodding.
When you go from Java to JavaScript or Python, you switch into a different mode of programming, where you look a lot more carefully at your code.

All you need is C or C++. All you need is a Turing machine, right? What we're talking about here is fundamentally a very personal, a very political, sort of a, it's almost a fashion statement about who you are, what kind of language you pick.
posts.key_insight: The future of language performance lies in runtime optimization, not static type systems - compilers will eventually get smart enough to make all the tricks programmers learned obsolete.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge argues that Joel Spolsky's famous 'Smart, and Gets Things Done' hiring heuristic is fundamentally flawed due to the Dunning-Kruger Effect—we can't reliably identify people smarter than ourselves in interviews. He proposes an alternative: 'Done, and Gets Things Smart,' meaning engineers who finish things impossibly fast AND improve systems while working on them. Drawing on his experiences at Geoworks, Amazon, and Google, Yegge emphasizes that successful tech companies need 'seed engineers'—freakishly brilliant people who establish engineering culture and can only be identified through extended work experience or reputation networks, not traditional interviews.
key_points: The Dunning-Kruger Effect makes it impossible to reliably identify candidates smarter than yourself in traditional interviews—you'll just hire clones of yourself
Having a good memory can actually impede understanding, letting you 'cheat your way through life' without developing real competence
Successful tech companies (Geoworks, Amazon, Google) all had brilliant 'seed engineers' who established lasting engineering culture
The only reliable ways to find exceptional engineers are working alongside them long enough to recognize their superiority, or asking around through reputation networks
'Done, and Gets Things Smart' engineers don't just complete tasks—they improve existing infrastructure and make systems smarter while doing their work
money_quotes: Having a good memory is a serious impediment to understanding. It lets you cheat your way through life.

It took me fifteen frigging years before I realized that there might in fact still be 'one or two things' left to learn, at which point I started looking outward and finally discovered how absolutely bambi-esquely thumperly incompetently clueless I really am.

How do you hire someone who's smarter than you? How do you tell if someone's smarter than you? This is a problem I've thought about, over nearly twenty years of interviewing, and it appears that the answer is: you can't. You just have to get lucky.
Interviews are conducted under pretty artificial conditions, and as a result they wind up being most effective at hiring people who are good at interviewing.
You can't do what Joel is asking you to do. You're not qualified. The Smart and Gets Things Done approach to interviewing will only get you copies of yourself.
For your startup, you don't want someone who's 'smart'. You want someone who's superhumanly godlike. Someone who can teach you a bunch of stuff.
These people aren't just pure gold; they're golden-egg-laying geese.
posts.key_insight: Traditional interviews fail because the Dunning-Kruger Effect prevents us from recognizing people smarter than ourselves—the only way to find exceptional 'seed engineers' is through extended collaboration or reputation networks.

posts.summary: Yegge argues that traditional business requirements gathering is fundamentally flawed because it results in building products nobody actually wants. He contends that successful companies build products their creators personally want to use, citing Warren Buffett and Peter Lynch's 'invest in what you know' philosophy as the business equivalent. Using examples from his time at Geoworks building the failed HP OmniGo and contrasting with the Flip camcorder's success, he demonstrates that trimming requirements leads to better products than gathering them. The core message: if you don't already know exactly what to build, you're in the wrong business.
key_points: Requirements gathering is a leading indicator of project failure because if you need to ask customers what they want, you've already lost
The best products come from building what you personally want and would use, not from focus groups or customer interviews
Simplification trumps feature expansion - successful products often come from taking things away, not adding them
Even great products can fail due to unpredictable forces like fashion, as illustrated by the mountain bike seat extender story
The Flip camcorder succeeded by ruthlessly trimming features to essentially one button and pretty colors
money_quotes: Business requirements are bullshit!
If you're planning to build something for someone else, let someone else build it.
If it's something you want, then you already know what the requirements are. You don't need to 'gather' them.
The GBR phase of a project is a leading indicator that the project will fail.
The easiest way to build a product that kicks ass is to start with someone else's great idea and take stuff away.
When you're trimming the business requirements, then you're exhibiting healthy project behavior. This contrasts directly with gathering requirements.
Don't gather business requirements: hire domain experts.
If you don't already know exactly what to build, then you're in the wrong business.
posts.key_insight: The need to gather business requirements is itself evidence that you don't understand your product well enough to build it successfully.

posts.raw: Here's the analysis of Steve Yegge's "Programming's Dirtiest Little Secret":

I need to read the actual blog post content. The post content shown appears to be truncated - it only shows the title, date, and an epigraph quote but no actual post body. Let me check if there's more content in the file.

posts.summary: Yegge announces Ejacs, a JavaScript interpreter he wrote in Emacs Lisp by porting Narcissus and implementing the full ECMA-262 runtime. The project stalled when he pivoted to js2-mode for JavaScript editing, which required porting Rhino's parser instead. The post morphs into a detailed comparison of Emacs Lisp vs JavaScript, where Yegge argues JavaScript is superior despite Elisp's macros. He criticizes Elisp's lack of namespaces, closures, delegation, properties, and proper serialization, while noting JavaScript has momentum and attracts smart people.
key_points: Ejacs required implementing a full ECMA-262 runtime because Narcissus cheats by calling JavaScript for its runtime
Emacs Lisp's global namespace problem forces verbose symbol prefixing and prevents real encapsulation
Dynamic scoping in Elisp means let-bound variables can be destroyed by callees, breaking encapsulation further
JavaScript's properties pattern is fundamental but requires significant work to implement in Elisp (hybrid alist/hashtable approach)
Smart people consistently prefer JavaScript despite being slightly embarrassed to admit it
money_quotes: Narcissus has a working parser and evaluator, but for its runtime it calls JavaScript. So it's kind of like saying you're building a car by starting from scratch, using absolutely nothing except for a working car.
Emacs Lisp is a 1984 Subaru GL 4WD: 'the car that's always in front of you.'
It pretty much combines the elegance of Common Lisp with the industrial strength of Scheme, without hitting either of them, if you catch my drift.
Emacs is basically one big program soup. There's almost no encapsulation to speak of, and it hurts.
Writing your own virtual method dispatch is just not something you should have to do in 2008.
I've found that JavaScript is a language that smart people like. It's weird, but I keep meeting really really smart people, folks who (unlike me) are actually intelligent, and they like JavaScript.
Implementer convenience is a pretty lame metric for deciding whether to support a feature, especially after 20 years of people asking for it.
Ejacs is just a toy, but I think it also shows a kind of promise. Scripting Emacs using JavaScript could open up the world's most powerful, advanced editing environment to millions of people.
posts.key_insight: JavaScript, despite its flaws, has momentum and attracts smart people, while Emacs Lisp's lack of closures, namespaces, and properties makes it verbose and poorly encapsulated—a language that 'gets the job done' but painfully.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge delivers a scathing review of Fable 2, a game he paid $160 for (including a network adapter to fix crashes). While acknowledging a few genuine highlights like the banshee enemies, the dog companion, and satisfying combat, he finds the game to be a poor Zelda clone drowning in juvenile humor, suffocating linearity, and baffling design choices. The review marks his departure from Xbox and PC gaming entirely, as he finds the game's creative direction inexcusably bad despite solid technical execution.
key_points: Fable 2 is a 'cargo-cult copy of Zelda' that misses the mark by relying on juvenile humor, constant hand-holding from an NPC named Theresa, and suffocating linearity at every level of design
The game's target audience is unclear - too crude for kids, too immature for adults, leaving only the conclusion it was 'designed by dipshits'
Technical execution (coding, artwork, sound) was great, but creative direction was terrible - a recurring theme of competent craft undermined by poor vision
The combat was satisfying but far too easy, removing any sense of fear or challenge from the experience
A few genuine highlights exist: the terrifying banshees, the believable dog companion, excellent architecture, and one riveting villain speech
money_quotes: It's a cargo-cult copy of Zelda that winds up having no identifiable soul: forgettable across the board.
The coding was great, the artwork was great, the sound effects were great; the details were for the most part rock-solid. But the creative direction was just inexcusably bad.
Your character can't so much as take a crap without Theresa piping in with helpful advice on which hand to use.
Dipshits. This game was designed by dipshits.
The combat was never hard, but it was on the whole fairly satisfying.
Busywork jobs only exist in MMORPGs to limit per-player CPU usage. They're not fun.
Amazing how Microsoft-run studios that are so consistently bad at humor are so good at creating convincingly evil speeches about taking over the world.
posts.key_insight: Technical excellence cannot save a game from creative direction that fundamentally misunderstands its audience and borrows mechanics without understanding why they worked in the original.

posts.summary: Yegge uses embedded systems and video games (particularly Mario Kart) as a framework for understanding the nature of boundaries in any system, including our universe. He explains that the 'invisible wall' at the edge of a game world represents a one-way frontier where the embedded system's rules simply end—there is no 'other side' in any meaningful sense from within that system. The concept of 'undefined' in programming becomes a metaphor for what lies beyond any system's boundaries: not 'nothing,' but 'anything'—a realm where normal rules don't apply and unpredictable phenomena can occur. This leads to the philosophical observation that all systems are embedded systems, including our universe, which has implications for questions about what existed before the Big Bang or beyond the universe's edge.
key_points: Embedded systems have one-sided frontiers where the rules of the system simply stop—asking 'what's on the other side' is semantically nonsensical from within the system
The 'undefined' space beyond a system boundary is not 'nothing' but 'anything'—a Wand of Wonder where unpredictable phenomena occur when programs accidentally cross it
All host systems are themselves embedded in larger systems, creating an infinite regression of containment
Channels through system frontiers (like game controllers or video output) allow information to flow across boundaries in predefined ways, but are often invisible from within
Questions like 'what happened before the Big Bang' are analogous to Mario asking what's beyond the invisible wall—well-formed mathematically but semantically nonsensical
money_quotes: The nothingness beyond the Invisible Boundary of an embedded system is much deeper and more significant than simply not having objects there. In that nothingness there is no programmatic substrate upon which to place objects.
Undefined does not mean 'nothing'; no, no no! It means anything. It is a modern-day Wand of Wonder, a Bag of Tricks, a Horn of Plenty, a Box of Chocolates. You never know what you're going to get.
If a computer program's code inadvertently reaches into Undefined, then it has stepped across a mystical wall into another universe, and anything could happen.
All host systems are embedded in some larger system. This is just the way things work.
Programmers already know intuitively the answer to these questions. The answer is: Undefined is there. Undefined is not the same as 'nothing', no indeed. It's anything. And we can't go that way.
The bugs are so spooky, and so hard to track down, that computers are now designed to be gatekeepers at the Wall.
posts.key_insight: The concept of 'undefined' in programming—where anything can happen when you cross system boundaries—provides a powerful mental model for understanding the inherent limits of asking questions about what lies 'outside' any system, including our universe.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge presents a collection of nine autobiographical vignettes inspired by another blogger he admires, davesecretary. The post is a departure from his usual technical content, instead offering humorous personal anecdotes ranging from embarrassing elevator encounters to giant spiders on his face to accidentally wearing his coworker's pants to Amazon. The stories share themes of physical comedy, awkward social situations, and the absurdity of everyday life. Yegge frames this as 'light reading' while he works on more technically ambitious posts, and the writing showcases his trademark self-deprecating humor and gift for comedic timing.
key_points: Yegge openly admits to being jealous of another writer (davesecretary) and decides to emulate his storytelling style rather than pretend otherwise
The recurring 'DEFECTIVE!' punchline appears twice - once when his brother crushes a chair and once when football fans tear out stadium bleachers
His father's observation that people mishear 'Y' as 'W' when spelling their last name took decades to discover but proves consistently true
The Amazon story reveals early company culture - a small building near a needle exchange with a teriyaki place employees called 'Scaryaki'
Yegge served in the Navy nuclear reactor program in Idaho before his tech career, hinting at a much more varied background than typical programmers
money_quotes: I'm guessing this is more or less what heroin is like. Some days I don't even shower.
He writes way better than I do, and I'm just going to copy him blatantly, like Terry Brooks rewriting Lord of the Rings line-by-line badly as Shitstorm of Shannara.
All he could think was: This chair is DEFECTIVE!
Falling-asleep voice and tiny voice were both, like, 'Oh, shit!'
Dave barges in, wild-eyed and soaking wet and yelling at us at the top of his lungs... 'DIDN'T ANY OF YOU HEAR ME? I WAS STUCK IN THE GARBAGE CAN!'
Jacob suddenly announces, in a really loud voice that everyone within a block or so can hear: 'You're wearing my pants!'
I said very carefully: 'e', and he wrote 'i', completing my last name as 'Wijji'. It was Jiffily Lubriciously Awesome.
posts.key_insight: Good storytelling requires honesty about influences and willingness to be vulnerable about embarrassing moments - the funniest stories come from situations where you looked like an idiot.

posts.summary: Yegge uses Dan Ariely's rejected credit card 'buckets' idea to illustrate a fundamental truth about complex projects: things that seem simple to imagine are monumentally difficult to implement. He traces how an elegant banking idea would balloon into thousands of questions about edge cases, user interfaces, and system integrations. He then applies this lesson to marijuana legalization, demonstrating that what seems like a simple policy change actually requires answering hundreds of specific implementation questions. The core argument is that 'Shit's Easy Syndrome' - the VP belief that anything imaginable is easily executable - ignores the crushing complexity of real-world implementation.
key_points: What VPs call 'simple ideas' become 5,000-page specs when programmers ask the necessary implementation questions
Banks didn't reject the credit bucket idea because of greed - they rejected it because the implementation complexity was astronomical
Laws are essentially programs that must be specified with similar precision, making legal reform a massive software-like project
Every 'simple' policy change like marijuana legalization spawns hundreds of edge cases: advertising rules, prison releases, licensing, interstate commerce, testing standards
Patience is required because complex systems take months, years, or decades to properly implement - overnight change is a pipe dream
money_quotes: YOUR IDEA IS INSANE. It doesn't even matter what it is. It's frigging insane. You are a nut case.
VPs have what my brother Mike refers to as 'Shit's Easy Syndrome.' You know. As in, shit's easy. If it's easy to imagine, then it's easy to implement.
Laws are pretty much like programs. You have to specify their behavior at almost the same level of detail, using a language that's almost as crappy as our programming languages today — English.
Amazon did teach me a valuable lesson, namely that YOUR IDEA IS INSANE.
Shit is NOT easy. Remember that. If you think it's easy, then you are being naïve. You are being a future VP. Don't be that way.
People will die. It's a certainty. Some people are going to die as a direct consequence of legalization of marijuana.
He's not a frigging King, he's a President. He can't make stuff happen overnight by waving his magical sceptre.
posts.key_insight: The gap between 'easy to imagine' and 'possible to implement' is where most ambitious projects go to die, whether in software, banking, or government policy.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge announces that his blog is winding down, with only three entries remaining after this one. This installment introduces part three of his 'Programmer's View of the Universe' series as an 18-page science fiction short story set 1000 years in the future. He notes the story will be controversial and offensive to many, emphasizing it doesn't represent his employer's views. The post serves primarily as a teaser for the linked story, with promises that future installments will explain the ideas in traditional blog-rant format.
key_points: Yegge announces he's ending his blog after just three more posts, planning to go anonymous or stop entirely
The story is set 1000 years in the future specifically as a critique of sci-fi's tendency to underestimate project timelines
He's self-aware about the incoming controversy, preemptively distancing himself from his employer
His final planned post about a programming language and Emacs is what he truly cares about, working on it despite physical pain
money_quotes: I'm old and embittered enough to know better. You probably shouldn't even read it. Just wait for someone to summarize it for you.

Unlike 1984, 2001, The Singularity is Near and all the other sci-fi stories that grossly underestimated their project durations, this one is set 1000 years in the future. I.e., right around the corner.
The story is disrespectful to pretty much everyone in the world. It will create a fantastic shit storm.
My final blog-rant entry is the only one I care about anymore. I've been working on it so hard that my fingers have started to fail.
This week is going to suck. People are going to be mad.
posts.key_insight: Even at the announced end of his blogging career, Yegge remains committed to provoking controversy while maintaining a self-aware, sardonic distance from the reactions he knows he'll provoke.

posts.summary: After a year-long blogging hiatus, Yegge returns to reflect on what he learned: caring too much about reader reactions was damaging his health, haters are inevitable (Reddit proves nothing gets above 80% approval), and his posts were indeed too long. The bulk of the post details his experience with Trigger Finger, a repetitive strain injury he acquired from obsessively practicing a Villa-Lobos guitar etude approximately 650,000 times. After cortisone shots and eventual surgery, his hand recovered to about 95%, teaching him the value of moderation in all pursuits. He plans to return to tech blogging with a lighter touch and shorter posts.
key_points: Haters are a statistical inevitability - even successful Reddit posts only reach 65-80% approval, so there's no point wasting energy on them
Trigger Finger is the second most common hand ailment after Carpal Tunnel, often caused by repetitive overuse in musicians and typists
The obsessive personality that drives someone to practice an arpeggio 650,000 times in 5 months is the same one that leads to overly long blog posts and caring too much about reader reactions
Physical therapy and surgery can't fully erase the fear - his hand still twists to avoid pain that's mostly gone, a metaphor for psychological damage
Forced moderation from injury led to positive changes: sustainable guitar style, better typing economy, and Emacs shortcuts he wishes he'd learned sooner
money_quotes: There is nothing on this earth that can make everyone happy.
Caring is fine. Lots of things are worth caring about. Very few of them merit sacrificing your health.

If they ever make a movie about my life, the handsome and dashing actor who plays me, when asked on his deathbed which of life's pleasures had given him the greatest happiness, will say something cheesy that makes the audience ooh and aww with appreciation, but it'll be total Hollywood bullshit, because what I really will have said was 'gaming'.

Its more formal name is digital tenovaginitis stenosans, which is ancient Latin for 'electronic hand inflamed vagina without writing', which I believe is why most people prefer to call it Trigger Finger.
Trigger Finger is 'idiopathic', a fancy word meaning that doctors don't have a fucking clue what causes it.
Your body will begin telling you when it's time to ease up on the practice sessions. Listen to your body when it says that.
In the last fifteen minutes my whole life had been handed back to me with an almost casual lack of concern.
My right-hand fingers twist and curl in elaborate, incomprehensible dances to avoid a pain that is for the most part no longer there.
posts.key_insight: The same obsessive drive that makes someone successful can also destroy them if they don't learn moderation - whether it's practicing guitar 650,000 times or caring too much about blog post reactions.

posts.summary: This satirical post mocks Java's excessive use of access modifiers (private, final, protected) and deprecation annotations by imagining Wikileaks 'liberating' open source Java code by removing these restrictions. Through fake interviews with fictional developers, Yegge argues that Java's encapsulation obsession contradicts the spirit of open source software. The piece contrasts Java's restrictive philosophy with Python's more permissive 'we're all adults here' approach. Ultimately, it's a critique of how Java culture prioritizes control over usability, even when the code is supposedly 'open.'
key_points: Java's private/final/protected modifiers often prevent legitimate use cases, even in open source code where users theoretically have full access
The @deprecated pattern in Java creates busywork where developers deprecate code just to force users onto newer, more complicated APIs
Python's philosophy treats users as adults who can make their own decisions, while Java's philosophy assumes users need protection from themselves
The Eclipse ecosystem exemplifies the worst of Java's over-engineering tendencies with pre-deprecated code and framework lock-in
Open source Java is philosophically contradictory: 'here's my code, but fuck you, use it exactly how I tell you'
money_quotes: Open Source does *not* mean the source is somehow 'open'.
If I make something private, it means that no matter how desperately you need to call it, I should be able to prevent you from doing so, even long after I've gone to the grave.
It's for your own protection.
Why can't users of my Open Source Java library simply shake their fists and curse my family name with their dying breaths?
If people could keep using the older, more convenient APIs I made for them, then why the fuck would they use my newer, ridiculously complicated ones?
We have a policy of releasing entire Java packages in which every single class, interface and method is deprecated right out of the box, starting at version 1.0.
When Python developers release Open Source code, they are saying: Here, I worked hard on this. I hope you like it. Use it however you think best.
Whereas Java developers who release Open Source code are saying: Here, I worked hard on this. I hope you like it. But use it exactly how I tell you to use it, because fuck you, it's my code.
posts.key_insight: Java's obsession with access control and deprecation reflects a paternalistic philosophy that fundamentally contradicts the collaborative spirit of open source software.

posts.summary: This satirical piece mocks both Haskell's lack of industry adoption and the programming community's resistance to learning it. Framed as a fake news story, it announces the 'discovery' of a single industry programmer who follows Haskell developments—bringing the total of people who care to 38. The post skewers Haskell's academic community for their jargon-heavy approach while simultaneously criticizing industry programmers for being too lazy to engage with the language. The humor works both ways, lampooning Haskell's impracticality and programmers' anti-intellectualism alike.
key_points: Haskell's adoption problem is framed as an NP-complete problem—mathematically impossible to solve
The '38 upvotes' joke suggests Haskell interest is a fixed constant independent of actual announcements
Industry programmers skip the learning part and go 'straight to the not using it part'
Haskell researchers are portrayed as out of touch, believing 'mutually recursive generic container types' are the adoption barrier
The post admits it contains 'deeper social subtext decrying the endemic superficiality and laziness of global industry programming culture'
money_quotes: Finding a person who gives a shit about Haskell is an inherently NP-complete computer science problem.
I'd have thought there would be more people following the press releases closely and then not using Haskell. But they all just skip the press releases and go straight to the not using it part.
People see words like monads and category theory, and their Giving a Shit gene shuts down faster than a teabagger with a grade-school arithmetic book.
We crafted a fake satirical post lampooning Haskell as an unusable, overly complex turd—a writing task that was emotionally difficult but conceptually trivial.
I believe the root cause of the popularity problem is Haskell's lack of reasonable support for mutually recursive generic container types.
Programmers only seem to pay attention to things when there is humor involved.
We do have an experimental humor monad. But it doesn't seem to be getting much adoption.
posts.key_insight: The gap between academic programming language research and industry practice is so vast that genuine interest in advanced languages like Haskell has become statistically anomalous—and both sides share the blame.

posts.summary: This satirical fake news piece mocks eBay and PayPal's notoriously frustrating checkout process by pretending they've patented their convoluted '10-click' buying system. Yegge skewers the disconnect between eBay's 'Buy It Now' promise and the reality of endless password prompts, upsells, and confirmation screens. The piece extends the joke by imagining other rejected patents for equally frustrating UI elements. It's a pointed critique of how dominant platforms can abuse their market position to deliver terrible user experiences without consequence.
key_points: eBay's 'Buy It Now' button is satirized as requiring anywhere from 8-15 clicks to actually complete a purchase
The piece mocks forced sign-outs, password juggling, upselling, and usury-like lending offers as deliberate friction
Other tech companies (Sony PSN, iPad App Store, Firefox) are called out for similarly broken UI promises
The 'monopoly' dig suggests dominant platforms have no incentive to improve user experience
money_quotes: logging in because they signed your sorry ass out again
any purchasing system that lies to you at least nine times about the 'Now' part of 'Buy It Now' is covered by their invention
eBay had tried to patent a purely decorative, non-operational 'Keep me signed in' checkbox
another eBay patent claim for excruciating page load times was rejected because the iPad App Store is still loading
the Firefox browser's 'Do this automatically from now on' checkbox has been complete bullshit for over fifteen years
eBay will continue to innovate on ways to remind their users that monopolies can do whatever the hell they want
eBay's stock rose on the news, driven largely by anonymous shill bidders
posts.key_insight: When companies achieve market dominance, they lose all incentive to respect users' time, turning simple actions into frustrating gauntlets of dark patterns and friction.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge humorously addresses a Hacker News misunderstanding where users thought he quit Google during an OSCON talk, when he actually just quit a specific project he wasn't passionate about. He uses this as a springboard to enthusiastically recommend working at Google while acknowledging their interview process is flawed. He reveals he's been working for 3.5 years on a project to turn source code into something like Wikipedia, and announces his intention to eventually pivot toward machine learning and cancer research.
key_points: Hacker News misinterpreted his OSCON talk, thinking he quit Google when he actually quit a project within Google
Google's interview process 'sucks at letting in all the qualified people' because interviewers want candidates exactly like themselves
He violated his own advice from his talk by working on something he wasn't passionate about, so he course-corrected
He's been working on a project to transform source code from plain text into 'Wikipedia' for 3.5 years
He's studying math and statistics to eventually work on machine learning problems, particularly cancer research
money_quotes: Google's interview process -- what's the word I'm looking for here -- ah yes, their process sucks at letting in all the qualified people.

The real problem is the classic interview process, which everyone uses and which Google hasn't innovated on, not really. It's like deciding whether to marry someone after four one-hour dates that all happen on the same day in a little room that looks kind of like a doctor's office except that the examining table is on the wall.
I'm getting old and I only have so many 'big projects' left that I can actually participate in.
It is a project that aims to turn source code -- ALL source code -- from plain text into Wikipedia.
It's a complete cop-out for me to take the easy path and work on a project that my company is excited about but I am not.
I've demonstrated to myself quite a few times that if I do anything daily for a few years I can get pretty good at it.
posts.key_insight: Life is too short to work on projects you're not passionate about, even if your company is excited about them.

posts.summary: Yegge analyzes why Borderlands became an enduring addiction despite being a seemingly simple shooter, arguing that its success stems from accidentally creating a perfect 'token economy' through its gun collecting system. He explains that fun alone doesn't create addiction—you need rarity-based collectibles, high score lists, and ways to display your collection. Gearbox stumbled into greatness through two critical bugs (the Armory glitch and ledge glitch) that enabled farming rare 'Pearlescent' weapons, combined with the Playthrough 2.5 endgame mode. The post predicts Borderlands 2 will be great but players will return to the original, and that an MMO version is inevitable.
key_points: Token economies—systems that reward behavior with visible, ranked tokens—are among the most powerful drivers of human behavior, used everywhere from schools to prisons to games
Fun gets people in the door, but addiction requires rarity, stack-ranking, display cases for collections, and personalization
Badge systems are 'what stupid people do when they think they've figured out Token Economies'—tokens need to be scored and ranked to create real addiction
Two accidental bugs (Armory glitch and ledge glitch) transformed Borderlands from a good game into an enduring phenomenon by enabling sustainable farming
The introduction of ultra-rare Pearlescent weapons—born from a color-coding bug players mythologized—gave collectors something worth grinding for years
money_quotes: Token economies are among the most powerful drivers of human behavior. They're used in grade schools, prisons, mental institutions and the military to incent people to act in certain ways. And it works, boy howdy does it ever work. Once they start handing out those gold stars, you'd shoot your own grandmother to get one.
Badge systems are what stupid people do when they think they've figured out Token Economies.

It's a fucking train wreck when the writers are juvenile, because they'll alienate everyone above their own level of sophistication—a demographic that just so happens to have the most disposable income to spend on games.

Fun alone won't keep people playing your game—both offline and online—four years later. Nope. In fact Fun has very little to do with the Magic Recipe for Addiction. All Fun can do for you is get people in the door.
Rarity creates desirability. It's hardwired into the human brain.
Jumping is fun. Period. End of story. If playing your game involves manipulating a humanoid ragdoll in three dimensions, and it doesn't support jumping, then you suck.
What the fuck good is collecting things if you can't show off your collection?
If raiding the Armory is like refined highbrow shopping at Saks Fifth Avenue, then Crawmerax's death is like a bomb going off in a Toys R Us.
posts.key_insight: Addiction in games (and products) comes not from fun but from token economies with rarity, ranking, and display—Borderlands succeeded because bugs accidentally enabled the perfect collecting ecosystem.

posts.summary: Yegge reviews Borderlands 2 as a masterpiece undermined by a single fatal flaw: insufficient storage for the game's 87 bazillion guns. Despite praising the writing, humor, voice acting, balance, and visual style as capital-G Great, he argues Gearbox fundamentally misunderstands their own token economy. The game creates gun collectors but gives them no way to collect guns, with only about 12 storage slots for an endgame built around farming legendary weapons. He predicts players will drift back to Borderlands 1 or resort to save-file modding, and calls for Gearbox to implement a proper gun database system rather than arbitrary inventory limits.
key_points: Borderlands 2 is a great game with outstanding writing, humor, balance, and the best bandit dialogue in gaming, but inventory management is a serious step backwards
The 87 bazillion guns marketing is meaningless when players can only keep about 12 of them - Gearbox created a collector's game without collection infrastructure
The game feels like a theme park with inconsistent visual design - too many saturated colors and disparate themed areas lacking cohesive vision
Eridium currency has no meaningful sink while players desperately need more bank slots - an obvious missed opportunity
True endgame farming requires a database-like system to manage hundreds or thousands of guns, not the current primitive inventory
money_quotes: Of course, the whole point of 87 Bazillion Guns is LOST, if you don't let people keep them!
They're the neighborhood kid who catches a lizard and thinks it's really cool, and it *is* cool, except he puts it in a box and it dies.

It's actually a great test of whether you're an idiot, because if you don't like it or it doesn't appeal to you... well, you might not be an idiot per se; there's bound to be some explanation... I guess.
Any three colors go together, and any more than that looks like a peacock shitting rainbows.
Crazed psycho bandits running at you screaming that Pluto is still a planet, or complaining about how goddamn cold it is outside, or reciting Hamlet... it just never gets old.
Gearbox has created a gun-collector's game, but they haven't given us a way to collect guns. How messed up is that?
Modding is stupid, stupid, stupid; it's 12 year olds advertising that they are, in fact, mentally and emotionally really twelve years old.
posts.key_insight: Creating a game centered on collecting 87 bazillion guns while only allowing players to keep a dozen of them reveals a fundamental misunderstanding of your own game's core appeal.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge returns to blogging after several years to announce his mobile MMORPG game Wyvern, which he's been developing for 20 years (originally started in 1996). He shares an amusing anecdote about his wife's pet monkey stealing her in-game loot, demonstrating how he used Jython's interactive evaluation to debug and fix the problem at runtime. The post compares iOS and Android development, strongly favoring iOS for its intuitive APIs and better monetization potential. He details his frustrating experience with Apple's App Store review process, including being rejected for IPv6 compliance issues and being forced to implement native account authentication. Despite the challenges, he remains optimistic about the game's future given his loyal player base.
key_points: Building a real MMORPG is an enormous undertaking - Yegge estimates hundreds of person-years of total effort, with significant contributions from volunteer area designers
iOS development feels familiar and intuitive compared to Android, which he describes as 'far, far, FAR from what you would expect' from any other UI framework
iOS monetization is 4-10x better than Android because Android has cultivated a 'frugal audience' through marketing and hardware choices
Apple's App Store review process is long and arbitrary - they can force major feature requests on developers with little recourse
Modern development infrastructure (GitHub, cloud platforms, Stack Overflow, contractor services) has dramatically increased productivity compared to the late 1990s
money_quotes: I'm pretty sure that in most states, accidentally replacing your wife's hard-earned treasure with bananas and bits of fur is legal grounds for divorce.
Normally you get a monster's inventory the old-fashioned way. You pay the iron price.
I always gauge how edgy my blog posts are by how likely I am to get fired over them, and my little indicator is redlining.
Twitter's philosophy is, roughly: 'Go fuck yourself.'
You're talking Java 1.0.2, with that O'Reilly book with the referees on the front. You had to roll everything yourself back then. Uphill both ways, in the snow.
But really, anyone can do it. You just gotta want it bad enough.
All I'm doing is copying code from Stack Overflow, really I have no shame whatsoever, and Xcode works great for that.
posts.key_insight: Building something meaningful requires enormous persistence over years or decades, but modern development infrastructure has made ambitious solo projects more achievable than ever before.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge recounts his journey from abandoning Android development due to painful Java APIs to falling unexpectedly in love with Kotlin. After a decade of searching for a Java replacement, he discovered Kotlin while attempting to port his game Wyvern to Android. Within weeks of experimenting, he was so impressed that he began rewriting his entire 20-year-old game server in Kotlin. He argues that Kotlin's killer app is Android itself—the platform's frustrating APIs created demand for a better language, and Kotlin delivers by being fully Java-interoperable while making code dramatically more pleasant to write.
key_points: Kotlin succeeded where other JVM languages failed because it offers seamless Java interoperability—you can mix Kotlin and Java in the same project and migrate incrementally
Android's notoriously bad APIs (Fragments, lifecycle management) created the perfect opportunity for Kotlin to thrive as a 'killer app' platform
JetBrains' background as IDE makers gave Kotlin unprecedented tooling support from day one, making it exceptionally easy to learn and use
Kotlin is practical and accessible to working programmers—it's not 'weird' like Scala or Clojure, but rather feels like a natural evolution from Java
The language restored Yegge's enjoyment of programming after years of Java-induced jadedness, making him feel like a hobbyist again
money_quotes: If the language you're using happens to be Java, then you've no doubt realized that by the time Java becomes a really good language, you'll be dead. Loooong dead.
I'm so jaded that I didn't think it was possible to love a language ever again, but Kotlin is just gorgeous.
Java 8 lets you say interesting things, but you have to do it with a mouthful of sand.
I just want street food, you know? Scala is nice but it's just too fancy for me, all frog legs and calf brains and truffled snails.

It only took maybe 3 days to learn Kotlin well enough to start busting out code, fully aware that I didn't know what the hell I was doing, but knowing the language and IDE were doing a great job of keeping me out of trouble anyway.
We hadn't fully grasped that programming had become non-fun until we tried Kotlin. It takes you back to when you were first learning programming and everything seemed achievable.
Kotlin manages to help you route around just about all of Android's Red Lights, and turns the experience into something that on the whole I now find superior to iOS development.
posts.key_insight: A new programming language can only succeed if it has a 'killer app' platform that makes developers willing to learn it, and Kotlin found that killer app in Android's frustratingly verbose Java APIs.

posts.summary: This is a redirect post announcing Steve Yegge's move from Blogger to Medium. The actual content about why he left Google to join Grab is hosted on Medium. This post serves only as a pointer for readers who followed his Blogger site.
key_points: Yegge migrated his blogging platform from Blogger to Medium
The substantive content about his Google departure exists only on Medium
This marks the end of his Blogger era
money_quotes: I've officially switched from Blogger to Medium.
posts.key_insight: This is a placeholder redirect post with no substantive content - the real article lives on Medium.

posts.summary: Yegge expresses strong skepticism about Kubernetes, arguing that despite being a simplified version of Google's internal Borg system, it still has an unnecessarily steep learning curve. He criticizes k8s for forcing users to learn concepts they shouldn't need to understand upfront, and suggests alternatives like Amazon Fargate or HashiCorp Nomad are better designed. He predicts the container orchestration battle is far from decided and doesn't believe in k8s's long-term dominance.
key_points: Kubernetes is a simplified clone of Google's Borg but still has an unacceptably steep learning curve
K8s forces users to learn unnecessary concepts upfront rather than providing good abstractions
Alternatives like Amazon Fargate and HashiCorp Nomad are better designed and potentially more scalable
The container orchestration market is still unsettled despite k8s's current popularity
money_quotes: Honestly I can't stand k8s.
It's a simplified clone of Google's internal borg system, but the learning curve is still just gross.
It forces you to learn all these concepts that you shouldn't need to worry about up front.
I think the battle for container orchestration is *far* from over, and I'm not a big believer in the future of k8s.
posts.key_insight: Good infrastructure tools should abstract away complexity rather than forcing users to understand implementation details upfront, and Kubernetes fails this test despite its popularity.

posts.summary: This is a science fiction short story, not a typical Yegge blog post. Set roughly a thousand years in the future, it follows the 'death' of Richard Dawkins—reimagined as a near-immortal saint of the 'Church of Modern Atheism'—during an extreme endurance race. The story weaves together multiple threads: two desperate Posey (poor human) boys stealing mechs to destroy Dawkins' data center, a tourist family experiencing the event as a historical tour, and Dawkins himself trapped in his biological race body. The narrative explores a post-Singularity world where most humans have migrated online, consciousness can be backed up, and the boundaries between life and death have become administrative matters.
key_points: The story imagines a future where atheism became an organized religion (COMA) with Dawkins as a canonized saint, which he opposed
Humanity has largely migrated online ('Rosies'), leaving only poor 'Poseys' in physical form, while 'Rivers' are conscious superorganisms evolved from social networks
The Singularity exists but is resource-limited and uses human interactions to generate truly random numbers via 'External Events'
Death has become complicated—Dawkins dies physically but exists as an 'External' entity that orchestrates mysterious phenomena
The ending suggests redemption as the boys who caused Dawkins' death are mysteriously granted enough money to become Rosies
money_quotes: Most of his long life, he reflected lazily, had been focused on other people. Educating them, mostly. It was a shame how often he'd had to resort to the switch, the rod, the metaphorical paddle. People just didn't want to learn.
Science advanced, technology advanced, but every generation was every bit as wilfully ignorant as its predecessors.
COMA hadn't killed theism. It had merely added itself to the pantheon for nine-tenths of the world's publicly comatheistic but privately polytheistic inhabitants.
Like all important races, it only ended when you died.
Being alone in his body felt absurdly like an out-of-body experience.
Between the Rivers and the Singularity, the demand for computing resources—with its consequent energy consumption and heat dissipation—had nearly killed the planet.
posts.key_insight: Even in a future where death is largely optional and atheism has become an organized religion, human nature—with its ignorance, desperation, and capacity for unexpected grace—remains fundamentally unchanged.

posts.summary: Yegge clarifies his earlier claim that Google has lost the ability to innovate, arguing this isn't necessarily fatal since most big companies don't innovate at scale. He outlines two valid alternatives: acquiring innovators (like Google buying YouTube) or building competing products quickly (like Microsoft's Xbox). Google's real problem isn't lack of innovation but poor execution on these alternatives—slow acquisitions, delayed market responses to Cloud and Social, and organizational complacency. He contrasts this with Jeff Bezos, who uniquely innovates relentlessly and iterates on failures until they succeed.
key_points: Most big companies don't innovate at scale, and that's normal—they can acquire innovators or build competing products instead
Google's actual problem is being too slow to respond to market shifts and too slow to execute when they do respond
Jeff Bezos is exceptional because he innovates constantly, doesn't fear failure, and keeps iterating until ideas succeed
Cookie-licking (claiming ownership of ideas without working on them) creates artificial scarcity of opportunity at big companies
Microsoft's Xbox success and later E3 failure illustrates how execution and customer connection matter more than innovation
money_quotes: Most big companies don't innovate at a large scale. It's normal.
Jeff Bezos is an exception. He innovates all the time, and he doesn't fear failure.
Many of his innovation attempts fall flat, but he almost never throws the idea away. Instead he keeps hammering at it until its form is the correct one for success.
Google's problem, in my personal opinion, is that although by and large they are not innovating (which, again, is normal for big companies), they also aren't doing the alternatives very well.
Google has an interesting problem of too much talent.
For any idea you propose, three or four teams will run in shouting that they own that, you can't touch it, and oh by the way, they're not working on it for a few years.
posts.key_insight: Innovation isn't required for big company success, but if you're not innovating like Bezos, you must excel at acquiring innovators or responding quickly to market shifts—and Google is currently failing at both alternatives.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge argues that software engineers should actively seek new jobs because global demand has outstripped supply, leading to unprecedented compensation packages and promotion opportunities. He revisits his famous Google interview advice, emphasizing that the fundamentals of computer science remain relevant after a decade. The post introduces a shocking discovery from recent interviews: multiple candidates from major tech companies couldn't perform basic arithmetic with large numbers like millions and megabytes. Yegge advocates for breadth-first interviewing to find knowledge gaps, while allowing one 'mulligan' for non-critical weaknesses. He concludes that knowing how to count—understanding orders of magnitude and computing units—should be non-negotiable for programmers.
key_points: The software engineering job market has gone 'crazy town' with demand completely outstripping supply, even running out of engineers in India for the first time ever
Big tech companies like Google are now 'coasting on their reputations' while offering bottom-quartile compensation compared to startups and unicorns
Breadth-first interviewing (probing many topics to find weaknesses) is more effective than depth-first (one hard question) because it's less random
Every candidate gets 'one mulligan' for a knowledge gap, but some skills must be non-negotiable—and basic counting/orders of magnitude is one of them
Multiple candidates from big-name companies couldn't tell the difference between megabytes and terabytes, or write the number one million correctly
money_quotes: Global demand for software engineers has completely outstripped supply.
However great you think your comp is, you should probably get out and talk to some recruiters, because someone out there is going to beat it significantly.
They have run out of engineers in India. How the hell do you run out of engineers in India?!
Interviewing someone is like trying to decide whether to marry them after a 1-hour date. It's not possible to get it consistently right.

Being a good interviewer is like trying to be a good detective — you need to look for clues, and follow up on them, and construct a complete picture of the tragic crime scene which comprises the candidate's abilities.
I sort of feel like making a mistake that's off by eight orders of magnitude is hard to attribute entirely to nerves.

If I ask you what you had for lunch, and you tell me that you're a little nervous but you're pretty sure you ate a hundred million sandwiches, then I think we probably have different definitions of 'nervous'.
posts.key_insight: The fundamentals of computer science—including the embarrassingly basic skill of understanding orders of magnitude—remain essential interview criteria, yet even engineers from top companies are failing these basics.

posts.summary: This post recounts Amazon's failed attempt to compete with eBay through their auction platform, symbolized by the forgotten mascot 'Mr. Tooth.' Yegge sets up a comparison between how Jeff Bezos and Jack Ma approached the challenge of breaking into eBay's locked market, suggesting Bezos got it 'spectacularly wrong' while Ma got it right. The post appears to be the beginning of a longer piece exploring the lessons from these contrasting approaches to market competition.
