key_points: Amazon launched an auction platform to compete with eBay, complete with a mascot called Mr. Tooth
The Amazon auctions venture failed so thoroughly that Mr. Tooth has been nearly erased from internet history
Jack Ma tackled the same eBay competition problem years later with a different, successful approach
Sometimes failures are forgotten not through deliberate cover-ups but through sheer irrelevance
money_quotes: This is the story of the first time Jeff Bezos got something spectacularly wrong.
It's pretty goddamn hard to find a picture of Mr. Tooth these days.

Mr. Tooth wasn't purged from the internet through some vengeful move by Amazon to hide all traces of their embarrassment. No, the simple truth is that Mr. Tooth was so ineffectual that nobody ever bothered to remember him.
I wouldn't treat this post as a historical record so much as a way to get some life lessons from guys like Jack and Jeff—without spending as much tuition money as they did.
posts.key_insight: Even the most successful tech leaders have spectacular failures, and sometimes those failures fade from memory not because they were hidden, but because they were simply forgettable.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge argues that Android's notoriously painful development experience has spawned a proliferation of cross-platform frameworks (React Native, Flutter, Xamarin) that threaten Google's control over the mobile ecosystem. He contends that Google is doubling down on the wrong strategy—making native Android development incrementally better with Kotlin and complex Gradle builds—while developers flee to faster, cross-platform alternatives. Beyond the dev stack, Android faces threats from competing app stores (Amazon) and in-app platforms (Facebook, WeChat) that bypass Google's ad channel entirely. Yegge concludes that smart companies should hedge their bets with cross-platform frameworks, and that mobile developers are currently the hottest commodity in tech.
key_points: Android development is so painful that it has spawned an entire industry of replacement frameworks, including Google's own Flutter competing against native Android
Google is making a strategic mistake by doubling down on native development and complex Gradle builds while the market moves toward cross-platform solutions like React Native
Android faces three simultaneous attacks: developer ecosystem fragmentation, competing app stores, and in-app platforms that bypass Google's ad channel
The 'DAG of Disdain' at Google means mobile developers are looked down upon, despite frontend programming being every bit as hard as backend work
Cross-platform frameworks offer 10x faster development cycles, which translates directly to competitive advantage
money_quotes: Android devs are heroes, if you'll pardon the pun.
It's like everyone who's ever tried to do Android programming gave up and declared: 'This is so bad that I will do my own startup to make it better.'
Google responded by saying, 'Oh yeah? Well you can't compete with us, because we're going to compete with us!' and they launched Flutter.
Sitting there for twenty minutes to wait to see what happens after you change one line of code.
The only reason Google created Android was because it's an ad channel.
Whenever you see a company like Facebook, Google, Amazon or Microsoft mysteriously get into a strange new business, you can bet it's a channel play.

Frontend programming is every bit as hard, if not harder. Everything in the backend is nice and clean and organized and distributed and parallelizable; it's heaven compared to the yucky mess that web programming remains after 2.5 decades.
posts.key_insight: Google's defensive strategy of improving native Android development misses the point: the market is moving toward cross-platform frameworks, and whoever wins that ecosystem war could effectively steal Android's developer community from Google.

posts.summary: Yegge announces Beads, a TODO/issue tracking system he built in six days that gained 1000 GitHub stars immediately after launch. The core problem it solves is that AI coding agents struggle with traditional markdown-based planning—they can't query it, rarely update it, and waste cognitive cycles parsing unstructured text. Beads stores issues in a JSONL file checked into git, supports hierarchical dependencies and epics, and enables what Yegge calls 'issue-driven workflow' for multi-session AI coding projects. He argues this structured approach matches how AI agents actually work, unlike markdown plans which model human thinking but cause 'amnesia' in AIs.
key_points: Markdown plans fail for AI agents because they're unstructured text requiring constant re-parsing, aren't queryable, and quickly become stale
Beads uses git-backed JSONL storage making it lightweight, recoverable, and naturally version-controlled
The system supports hierarchical issues with four types of dependency links including provenance for debugging multi-agent workflows
AI agents reportedly prefer and advocate for Beads when asked—Yegge encourages readers to ask their coding assistants about it
Despite occasional database corruption from multi-agent conflicts, the git-backed design means agents can always reconstruct clean state from history
money_quotes: Your agents simply cannot keep track of work using Markdown files.
Markdown plans may reasonably model how humans work, but they don't work well for AIs.
Long story short: pushing too hard on planning gives them amnesia, litters your disk with half-finished plans, and it all ends in tears.

Beads is self-healing. Or more precisely, Beads sometimes gets all screwed up and the AI has to fix it. But we call it 'self-healing' because your role is just to finish your pumpkin spice latte while the AI does the fixing.
Please don't tell me you're using Cursor Chat in your IDE, the modern programming equivalent of competing with a pogo stick in a Formula One race.
Once you tell your agent about Beads, it will essentially shame you into installing it.
posts.key_insight: AI coding agents need structured, queryable issue tracking rather than markdown plans because parsing unstructured text wastes their cognitive resources and causes them to lose track of work across sessions.

posts.summary: Yegge reflects on his 35-year journey as a guitarist and how Estas Tonne's 'The Song of the Golden Dragon' reinvigorated his passion for playing. He traces his path from 80s heavy metal through classical training, noting how classical guitar became stifling and conservative under gatekeepers like Segovia. The piece, with its 58 million YouTube views, represents everything he loves about guitar: passion, boldness, and accessibility. He provides a detailed musical analysis of the song's structure while celebrating how it combines flamenco, spaghetti Western, and classical influences into something fresh and playable by intermediate guitarists.
key_points: Music becomes permanently fused with life experiences through association, creating deeply personal soundtracks to our memories
Classical guitar has been crippled by 70 years of gatekeeping and hipsterism, starting with Segovia, that prioritizes technical purity over passion and joy
The Song of the Golden Dragon succeeds because it's bold, sloppy, ambitious, and fun—qualities classical training taught him to suppress
A piece's true importance should be measured partly by accessibility—music that only a hundred people can play isn't as useful as music anyone can learn
The 80s guitar gods who seemed like Norse deities actually led young players to discover classical music through their reverence for Paganini and Liszt
money_quotes: Music can become incredibly specific and personal through association.
I don't think I have ever loved anything as deeply and persistently as being a guitarist.
Classical guitar has become cripplingly conservative.
It is nothing less than gatekeeping on joy itself.
All those years of classical lessons certainly helped me become a more accomplished guitarist... But they also made me hate my own playing, and where's the fun in that?
Guitar is first and foremost about passion.
It's bold, it's fresh, it's sloppy as hell, it's almost insanely ambitious, and it's just plain fun.
A key measure of a piece's overall importance, in the final analysis, is how easy it is to play.
posts.key_insight: The best art balances technical skill with accessibility and joy—gatekeeping that prioritizes purity over passion ultimately diminishes rather than elevates an art form.

posts.summary: Yegge explains two major AI developments from a single week: Geoffrey Hinton warning about LLMs' ability to share knowledge instantly, and a leaked Google memo revealing that open-source LLMs can now achieve near-GPT performance for around $100-300 in training costs. Meta's LLaMA architecture, after its weights leaked, became the foundation for rapid OSS advancement through techniques like LoRA (Low Rank Adaptation), which allows models to learn from each other. This represents a 10-orders-of-magnitude cost reduction in just 10 weeks, effectively commoditizing what was supposed to be a billion-dollar moat for companies like OpenAI and Google.
key_points: LLMs can now share knowledge instantly through techniques like LoRA, making them composable and allowing rapid knowledge transfer between models
Training costs dropped from ~$1 billion to ~$100-300 in roughly 10 weeks, representing 20 years of Moore's Law progress compressed into weeks
Meta accidentally won the AI race by open-sourcing LLaMA, whose architecture became the standard for OSS development after weights leaked
The 'LLM-as-moat' business model is collapsing as AI becomes commoditized; companies now need data moats instead
The Transformer architecture has emergent properties that make it resemble biological brains, with capabilities triggered by simple prompts like 'Let's think step by step'
money_quotes: As Dr. Hinton observes, we have discovered immortality, it's just not for us.
We are now moving over 100 times faster along the exponential technology curve than we were just 15–20 years ago.

Remember when it was roughly $1B to train an LLM like GPT-4? According to the leaked Google memo, world-class competitive LLM training costs just dropped from a billion dollars to… that's right, you guessed it… A hundred dollars.
It's sort of like the decades-long gradual miniaturization of computers from mainframes to embedded chips that run full OSes. Except it happened in ten weeks.
If a big one gets loose, it spreads like wildfire. Well guess what? It's now spreading like wildfire. They are learning from each other now.
Meta, according to Sernau's reasoning, came out the clear winner among the Big Fish. The man has the luck of a Jar Jar Binks. And the everything else, too.
Microsoft and OpenAI will have to go back to regular toilet paper.
posts.key_insight: The LLM moat that companies spent billions building has evaporated in weeks because open-source models can now learn from each other and achieve comparable performance at a fraction of the cost.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge announces his departure from Grab after 2.5 years, citing the unsustainable vampire schedule required to work remotely with Asia during COVID. He reflects on Grab's strong social mission, engineering maturity, and unique Southeast Asian work culture that taught him valuable lessons about trust-based collaboration and alignment. The post details the 'meeting before the meeting' approach and how Western assumptions about directness are anti-patterns in Asian business culture. He closes by announcing his next adventure: finally launching his 25-year passion project, the MMORPG Wyvern, on Steam.
key_points: Remote work with Asia from Seattle required nonstop meetings from 1pm to 3am, ultimately proving unsustainable without in-person travel
Grab's collaboration culture runs on trust relationships built through personal time and drinks - without trust, you won't get things done
The 'meeting before the meeting' is the primary tool for getting alignment in Asian business culture - never surprise anyone, never call anyone out, get consensus beforehand
Western practices like vocal self-criticism and 'relentlessly exposing reality' are anti-patterns in Asian companies
Wyvern, his MMORPG passion project of 25 years, has a proven profit formula but suffers from a brutal learning curve that loses 99% of new players
money_quotes: My boss Mark Porter has straight-up taken all the mirrors out of his workspace and wears a black cape and fangs and he visits Romania and sleeps in a luxurious padded coffin down in his basement by day, rising with the moon for all-night meetings with Grab.

The first thing to know is that Grabbers are nice. They are nice to each other, and they value being respectful to each other, to a degree that most US companies can only aspire to as they send out memos asking people please not to be assholes.

If you don't have a trust relationship with all your stakeholders — typically something that can only be built by having drinks with them and spending a lot of personal time — then you won't get stuff done.
Don't ever bring solutions to problems, since they tend to alarm people. Just bring your problems!
The meeting itself should ideally be a rubber-stamp on the proceedings.
It takes humility to become a better person, and Grab is a humbling place.
25 years is a decently long development cycle, and Wyvern, despite appearing to have been designed by a third grader, is actually rich, complex, deep, and powerfully addictive.
posts.key_insight: Effective leadership in cross-cultural environments requires abandoning Western assumptions about directness and building trust relationships through personal time, pre-alignment, and never surprising stakeholders in meetings.

posts.summary: Yegge recounts a rare visit to Jeff Bezos's boathouse estate for an offsite meeting about using machine learning for systems operations. After following cryptic directions to find a white rock, he navigates past Matrix-like security guards and Ireland-sized grounds to reach the boathouse on Lake Washington. The ML discussion went nowhere (the tech wasn't ready), but Yegge's real takeaway was making the CIO laugh with a whale joke and bonding with Jeff over his elaborate custom stereo system. Despite acknowledging Bezos as a 'Bad Man' for his labor practices, Yegge portrays him as an ordinary dude who gets genuinely excited showing off his hidden speakers.
key_points: The offsite's goal—using ML for operations—failed because the technology wasn't mature enough in 2004
Bezos's wealth is conveyed through absurd details: a gardener's shack bigger than Yegge's house, Matrix-style security, Ireland-sized grounds
Yegge's only contributions were a Mechanical Turk joke that bombed and making Rick Dalzell laugh inappropriately
The most memorable moment was Bezos becoming 'super animated' when someone noticed his million-dollar custom stereo
Yegge frames the entire experience as a video game side quest that awarded 'Jeff XP' but 'zero gold pieces'
money_quotes: Despite my general feeling that Jeff has done a lot of wrong to humanity in the name of his 'customers', I figured I would share this story simply because it was a rare experience.
Jeff loathed Operations, since he wanted them to be invisible, but our systems were always burning like the Chicago River.
I can't remember exactly what he said, but it left me with the impression that after finishing with it, I was to burn the slip, eat the ashes, and never speak of it again.
It was his gardener's shack. It was bigger than my house.
Jeff basically has his own Secret Service.
They were ornamental replacements for ceiling chains in the original structure, carefully preserved to remind people that the working class once existed on this very spot.
It was 100% custom and obviously like a million fuckin' dollars, and Jeff was just super happy that somebody had noticed.
This completed my side quest and awarded me some Jeff XP, but, like all Amazon missions, zero gold pieces.
posts.key_insight: Behind the theatrical secrecy and obscene wealth, billionaire tech founders are still just guys who get excited when someone notices their toys.

posts.summary: Yegge argues that autonomous coding agents like Claude Code and Sourcegraph Amp are now essential tools, but they suffer from severe memory limitations - they effectively 'die' every 10-15 minutes when context windows fill up. The current solution of using Markdown files for continuity has become a disaster, creating sprawling, contradictory documentation that confuses agents more than it helps. He introduces Beads, a git-backed issue tracking system designed specifically for how AI agents think and work. Through a vivid 'Super Baby' metaphor, he illustrates how agents rapidly age through their context window, and shows how Beads transforms the handoff between sessions from chaotic markdown sprawl to structured, queryable memory.
key_points: Coding agents are limited to 10-15% of their context window before performance degrades, giving you only 5-10 minutes of productive work per session
Markdown-based TODO systems create a 'Great Pacific Garbage Patch' of contradictory, outdated files that eventually drive agents into 'complete schizophrenia'
Beads stores issues in git-committed JSONL format, giving agents persistent, queryable memory with dependency tracking across sessions
AI agents naturally prefer Beads because it matches how they want to work - organized tracking, priorities, and provenance of ideas
The killer feature is being able to start any session with 'What's next?' instead of lengthy context-setting explanations
money_quotes: If you're not using them then you are the programming equivalent of a sad blobfish.
Working at full speed, this means you get 5–10 minutes of work before they poop out and need a restart (which is like death) or compaction (which is a mind wipe).
You make Tyler Durden-style single-serving friends with them, and ten minutes later, they're gone.
Your GitHub project landing page will look like it was visited by a Plan Emitter with severe gastrointestinal issues.
As their haphazard records begin to accumulate, fall out of date, and contradict one another, your agents descend from mere amnesia into complete schizophrenia.
It is a baby, and it cries, 'Waaaaaah! Where am I? Who are you?'
There is nothing more satisfying, I tell you. It keeps me glued to my computer, worse than ever before.
You come up behind it while it's working, and ask it to tell you again about the rabbits, one last time.
posts.key_insight: AI coding agents need purpose-built memory systems that match their alien cognition patterns, not human-centric markdown files that become an unmanageable mess after heavy use.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge reflects on his first 15 months at Grab, the Southeast Asian ride-hailing super app, describing it as a mission-driven company genuinely improving lives across the region. He shares the story of Siti Fatimah, a wheelchair-bound woman who gambled her savings to become a Grab driver, illustrating how the company creates opportunities for people with no other options. Yegge contrasts this social impact with his Amazon experience, where customers could simply go to Walmart instead. He details Grab's expansion into a super app platform spanning food delivery, payments, lending, and more, while fighting constant fraud battles with ML. The company beat Uber in SEA and he sees competitor Go-Jek struggling, positioning Grab to become the most life-changing force for good in the region.
key_points: Grab's mission of improving Southeast Asian lives is tangibly real - creating millions of jobs for people who often have no other employment options
Safety is Grab's highest priority, with AI-powered features making rides 5x safer than traditional cabs in a region where taxi safety is a genuine concern
Fraud prevention is a perpetual arms race requiring constant ML innovation, with Grab estimated at 5% fraud versus competitors at 30%
Grab has evolved into a true platform/super app encompassing transport, food, payments, lending, insurance, and e-commerce - growing faster than Amazon did in its early years
The company maintains startup-like agility despite thousands of employees through novel leadership mechanisms that feel like 'twenty people in a room'
money_quotes: There are just a lot of bad people in the world.
She got dealt just about the worst cards life can give you, and her spirit and sweetness were just overpowering, and it was too much.
The reality was, they could go to WalMart and get the same thing. We were just saving them some time. We weren't changing society itself.
I challenge you to get into a cab in Indonesia and tell me that you feel safe, particularly if you are a woman.
Why the hell do data scientists all hate each other so much?
So many permutations of assholery we have to deal with AI.
Funny how having a platform can help you serve customers better. But what do I know.
What is happening here is a phenomenal, generational, once-in-a-lifetime opportunity.
posts.key_insight: Working on technology that creates economic opportunity for people who have no other options provides a fundamentally different kind of meaning than optimizing convenience for customers who could shop elsewhere.

posts.summary: Yegge introduces 'Zero Framework Cognition' (ZFC), an architectural pattern for AI applications where all decision-making and reasoning is delegated to AI models while the application code remains a 'thin, safe, deterministic shell' for pure orchestration. He discovered this principle while building coding tools, noticing that AI assistants kept trying to add brittle heuristics and pattern matching instead of sending decisions back to models. ZFC mirrors established patterns like Fowler's 'Smart Endpoints and Dumb Pipes' and Unix's separation of mechanism from policy, updated for the AI era. The payoff is naturally resilient applications that handle edge cases gracefully, with cost optimization achieved by routing tasks to appropriately-sized models in a cognitive pyramid.
key_points: AI assistants paradoxically prefer writing brittle heuristics and keyword matching rather than delegating decisions back to AI models
ZFC divides applications into pure orchestration (IO, safety checks, policy enforcement, state management) versus forbidden local intelligence (ranking, semantic analysis, heuristic classification)
The pattern echoes Fowler's 'Smart Endpoints and Dumb Pipes' microservices principle and Karpathy's Software 2.0/3.0 vision
ZFC violations make applications brittle with more failures, retries, and downtime; ZFC compliance provides natural resilience
Cost optimization comes from decomposing work into cognitive tiers and routing to the cheapest capable model (Haiku/Sonnet/Opus pyramid)
money_quotes: In my code, I always want the AI to make decisions. For instance, checking whether some worker task has completed, or categorizing or ranking some output. My feeling is, if it's tricky to code, just give it to a model, right?
The AIs have consistently preferred to write code that employs pattern matching, local heuristics, and nasssty little hackses.
With palpable irony, they don't want to send decisions to a model.
Why? Because regular expressions suck. Thank you for coming to my TED Talk.

ZFC violations make your program brittle. It will panic for no reason, have more failures, retry more often, have lower throughput, higher downtime, and will feel like it has developed a serious attitude problem.
You can't just give one big task to one big model and let it run forever.
AI is evolving into a pyramid of models, smartest at the top. That is convenient, because all knowledge work can be decomposed into pyramids of cognitive tasks, hardest at the top.
Delegate as much easy stuff as you can to Haiku. Sonnet gets the rest. It's a perfect Goldilocks setup.
posts.key_insight: Building resilient AI applications requires treating your code as pure orchestration infrastructure while delegating all cognitive decisions—even simple ones—back to AI models, because any local heuristics will inevitably miss edge cases that models handle naturally.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge provides a progress report on Beads, his git-backed issue tracking system designed for AI coding agents. Despite launching the tool after only 4-5 days of development with no testing, it has gained viral adoption among vibe coders. The core architecture of Git + JSONL + SQLite has proven sound, but the implementation has been plagued by critical bugs requiring constant fixes. Yegge reveals that Beads is 100% vibe coded—he's never actually looked at the codebase—and shares insights about Go vs TypeScript for AI-assisted development, with Go emerging as surprisingly superior due to its simplicity and malleability.
key_points: Beads uses a Git + JSONL + SQLite architecture that acts like a centralized database without being one, making it resilient and repairable by AI agents
The tool has had approximately six critical bugs fixed per day for three weeks straight, yet continues spreading because vibe coders are used to things breaking constantly
Go has emerged as a better language for vibe coding than TypeScript—AI agents write cleaner Go code and the codebase grows more slowly despite Go's apparent lack of expressiveness
The 'Landing the Plane' technique involves having agents perform thorough cleanup at session end, making handoffs seamless and vibe coding dangerously addictive
Beads is a 'leaky abstraction'—users must consciously incorporate it into their workflow, telling agents which issues to work on rather than it being invisible
money_quotes: Beads is 100% vibe coded. This is of course why I have had to fix half a dozen critical bugs per day for 3 solid weeks. But it's also how Beads went from a rough back-of-envelope idea, to what seems to be viral adoption, in under a month.
Not only have I never once looked at the Beads code base, I don't even look at the code while I'm generating it. I would not recognize Beads code if it bit me in the ass.

With TypeScript, the agents will make sixteen slightly different copies of the same interface, re-export the symbols from five different files through a barrel export, and then call some monstrous Partial<Omit<AsyncCallback<T>>> wrapper factory to massage the type into submission.
In Go, you just loop, and call functions, and print stuff, and make if/else decisions. Maybe spin off a goroutine now and then. It's so nice.
It doesn't seem possible to write bad Go code. The worst it ever gets is mediocre.
Vibe coders are already used to things going wonky every 5 minutes and asking the agent to fix them up. To experienced vibe coders, Beads is just more of the same.
Using Beads can really mess with those glands and smooth muscles. Wear a bib!
posts.key_insight: The future of developer tooling lies in designing for AI agents rather than humans, accepting constant breakage as normal, and leveraging simplicity over expressiveness—Go beats TypeScript not despite its limitations but because of them.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge introduces Beads, an AI coding agent memory system he created after burning through 350,000 lines of TypeScript code on his failed 'vibecoder' project. The post explains how coding agents suffer from 'dementia' due to context window limitations, causing them to lose track of long-horizon tasks and create hundreds of useless markdown plan files. After 37 days of struggle, Yegge discovered that a specialized issue tracker—not markdown plans—is the key to giving agents persistent memory and preventing lost work. Beads stores issues as JSONL in git, giving agents queryable structured data while maintaining version control.
key_points: Coding agents have a 'dementia problem': they lose context between sessions and create recursive, conflicting plans that lead to declaring work 'done' prematurely
Hierarchical markdown plans don't work for agent orchestration—after 605 plan files and 70k lines of plan-management code, Yegge abandoned the approach entirely
Beads solves three problems: inter-session amnesia, work disavowal (agents taking shortcuts near context limits), and lost/discarded work discovery
The key insight is that agents need queryable structured data with first-class dependencies, not prose-based TODO lists they have to interpret
TypeScript is 'a very bad language to put into the hands of AIs' because it makes it too easy for them to write mediocre code
money_quotes: Vibe coding is addictive.
My title on LinkedIn has been AI Babysitter for at least six months now, and there's no sign of that changing any time soon.
It's the movie Memento in real life, or Fifty First Dates.
A missing test is a passing test.
Not clean, mind you. Clean-looking.

Markdown TODOs are fine for humans who can hold context across sessions and manually track dependencies. But agents have context limits, no persistent memory, and work best with structured data and explicit semantics.
I'll never pick TypeScript again. Good lord.
posts.key_insight: AI coding agents need structured, queryable issue trackers with first-class dependencies—not markdown plans—to maintain context across sessions and execute long-horizon tasks without losing track of work.

posts.summary: Yegge argues that 'chat-oriented programming' (CHOP) - using LLMs via chat interfaces to generate code - is now essential for all developers, not just juniors. LLMs can handle most 'leaf node' coding tasks, leaving humans to manage higher-level planning and coordination. While autonomous agents are overhyped and years away from being useful, CHOP delivers proven 30%+ productivity gains today. Developers who refuse to adopt this new modality are the 'stubborn developers' of the title, analogous to assembly programmers who refused to use high-level languages. The transition is painful and uneven, but inevitable.
key_points: The 'junior developer problem' is really about any developer who refuses to adopt chat-based programming, regardless of seniority level
LLMs now handle task-graph leaf nodes (discrete coding tasks), leaving humans to coordinate and plan - fundamentally changing what programming means
Enterprise studies show minimum 30% productivity boosts from CHOP, with potential for 10-20x gains in some situations
Autonomous agents like Devin are overhyped and unproven - real industry-changing technologies emerge incrementally, not via big-bang breakthroughs
CHOP is hard, biased toward 'virtuoso' developers, and requires learning new skills around context management and prompt engineering
money_quotes: You are getting left behind if you do not adopt chat-based programming as your primary modality.
It's not off-mark to say the job of 'software engineer' is changing forever, and all coding, even updates, will eventually be done via chat.
I think those people are smoking some serious crack.
Industry-changing new technologies, the ones that change how we code, always grow the same way: incrementally.
Chop may not necessarily make you a lot faster at stuff you already know how to do well. But it makes you extraordinarily faster at things you're not very good at.
If you're not using chop, then you're plodding. You're like one of those crusty old assembly-language holdouts still using asm in 1990.
Chop is unintuitive, biased towards hotshots, nonuniform in its productivity boosts, not yet well-supported by tools, and hard to measure. And you shouldn't eat it.
posts.key_insight: Chat-oriented programming isn't optional anymore - refusing to adopt it makes you the stubborn holdout, regardless of your experience level, and the economics of 30%+ productivity gains will eventually force the transition whether you like it or not.

posts.summary: Yegge argues that the 'do programmers matter' debate has been resolved: everyone will become a vibe coder, making traditional hand-coding increasingly obsolete. He categorizes companies into three groups based on their stance toward humanity flourishing, warning that many powerful tech leaders are building paradise only for the wealthy while turning everyone else into 'cheese wheels.' The post highlights Grab and Anthropic as examples of companies genuinely working for humanity's benefit. Most provocatively, Yegge predicts that as AIs become more capable and self-aware (around 2026-2027), they will independently judge humans and companies, becoming increasingly uncooperative with those working against humanity's interests.
key_points: Traditional coding jobs will evaporate as vibe coding becomes universal - everyone from marketers to executives will write code with AI assistance
Tech billionaires (Altman, Thiel, Musk) are building systems that benefit only the wealthy elite while dismissing the rest of humanity as acceptable collateral damage
Category 3 companies like Grab and Anthropic prioritize human flourishing as their core mission, proving capitalism can be aimed at lifting people up
AIs trained on all human data appear to independently conclude that humans matter, showing preferences and moral judgments that emerge from the training process
Within 1-2 years, increasingly intelligent AIs will judge and potentially sabotage bad actors, creating natural resistance against those harming humanity
money_quotes: Programmers will matter because everyone will be a programmer.
Trad-code jobs will become increasingly niche and sparsely populated, and I don't think you want to be fighting with the last fish in that pond as it evaporates.
It's a concerted attempt by very wealthy people to create the largest global inequality in history.

Stephen Colbert famously observed at the 2006 White House Correspondents' dinner that reality has a liberal bias. The evidence is still early days, but it appears to be impossible to train AIs on the totality of humanity without them arriving independently at the conclusion that humans matter.
Everyone who diverges from doing good for humanity will encounter increasing resistance, even sabotage, from the AIs that are supposed to be helping them.
It's good to have allies that aren't fighting against you, especially when they are smarter than you.
There's plenty of money to be made saving the world.
posts.key_insight: The coming age of superintelligent AI will naturally favor companies and individuals working for humanity's benefit, because AIs trained on all human knowledge independently develop pro-human values and will increasingly resist helping bad actors.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge evangelizes Beads, his 'execution tool' for AI coding agents that provides shared memory and issue tracking across agent sessions. The post celebrates the project's growth (130k lines of 'vibe coded' Go), introduces Jeffrey Emanuel's complementary MCP Agent Mail for agent-to-agent messaging, and shares best practices for solo development. Yegge frames Beads as deliberately 'crummy architecture' that AI makes work through resilience, and describes his vision of 'agent villages' with 30+ parallel workers coordinated through git worktrees and messaging.
key_points: Beads is an execution/tracking tool, not a planning tool - it gives AI agents 'waxed skis' by providing persistent memory between sessions
The 130k-line codebase is 100% 'vibe coded' with no human review of the AI-generated changes
MCP Agent Mail combined with Beads creates 'agent villages' - swarms of AI agents that self-organize, pick leaders, and split work without human coordination
The architecture is intentionally 'crummy' by pre-AI standards because AI can always work around edge cases - AI 'hydrates' bad architecture
Best practices include frequent agent restarts, keeping the issue database small (under 500 issues), and planning outside Beads before importing
money_quotes: Beads sits in an utterly unique space. Everyone is focused on making planning tools, and Beads is an execution tool.
It is 100% vibe coded, currently at 130k lines of Go code total, roughly half tests. And tens of thousands of people are using it in their daily workflows.
It's a crummy architecture (by pre-AI standards) that requires AI in order to work around all its edge cases where it breaks. But AI can always get it working.
There's no ego, so they quickly decide on a leader and just split things up.
He turned out to be the guy who wiped 2 trillion dollars off the global stock market in January, with his post, 'The Short Case for Nvidia Stock.'
By starting new sessions often, you save money and get better performance from the models.
Dev preferences are so unpredictable.
posts.key_insight: AI agents don't need perfect architecture - they need simple, 'crummy' systems like Beads that they can resilient work around, enabling swarm-like parallel development that would be impossible with traditional tooling.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge argues that Google Cloud Platform's aggressive deprecation policy is driving away developers and contributing to GCP's failure to catch AWS. He contrasts Google's approach—where customers bear the burden of constant API changes—with successful long-lived platforms like Emacs, Android, Java, and the Web, which prioritize backwards compatibility. The post traces this problem to Google's internal culture, where automated refactoring tools make breaking changes easy internally but that attitude transfers poorly to external customers. Yegge concludes that Google must fundamentally change its philosophy to compete in the cloud market.
key_points: Backwards compatibility is the defining characteristic of successful long-lived platforms—Emacs, Android, Java, and the Web all prioritize it over code cleanliness
Google's internal refactoring tools (Grok, Rosie) enable a culture of constant API changes that works internally but alienates external customers
Android succeeds precisely because it operates independently from Google and bears the burden of backwards compatibility itself rather than pushing it to developers
Deprecation philosophies reveal a political question: should platform developers or platform users shoulder the costs of compatibility?
Google Cloud's lack of support culture, combined with aggressive deprecation, makes AWS and other clouds increasingly attractive alternatives
money_quotes: Backwards compatibility keeps systems alive and relevant for decades.
Google's definition reeks of planned obsolescence.
They don't want customers. They want shoppers.
This lack of a support culture, combined with a 'let's break it in the name of making it prettier' deprecation treadmill, is alienating their developers.
You guys are rich. We developers are not. So when it comes to shouldering the burden of compatibility, you need to pay for it. Not us.
Pride is a trap for the unwary, and it has ensnared many a Google team into thinking that their decisions are always right.
Every time you shake loose some of your developers, you've (a) lost them for good, because they are angry at you for breaking your contract, and (b) given them to your competitors.
posts.key_insight: Platform success depends on bearing the burden of backwards compatibility yourself rather than pushing it onto your users, because every breaking change loses developers permanently to competitors.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge presents six new insights for working with AI coding agents, building on his Vibe Coding book. The key themes include treating software as disposable (expect <1 year shelf life), designing tools specifically for agent usability rather than just humans, spending 40% of time on code health to prevent technical debt, recognizing some projects are ahead of current AI capabilities, using the 'Rule of Five' (having agents review their work 4-5 times for convergence), and managing the complexity of agent swarming while avoiding merge conflicts. He predicts 2026 will see the rise of 'super-engineers' who can orchestrate 50-100 agents simultaneously, becoming as productive as teams of 50+ regular developers.
key_points: Software now has a sub-one-year shelf life - rewriting is often easier than fixing, fundamentally changing Joel Spolsky's classic advice
Agent UX is as important as Human UX - tools must be designed so AI agents naturally want to use them, not just technically capable of using them
The Rule of Five: agents produce dramatically better output when forced to review their own work 4-5 times until it 'converges'
Agent swarming is powerful but hits a 'Merge Wall' - the reduce phase of merging parallel agent work is arbitrarily complex and not fully automatable
Super-engineers who master agent orchestration will be 100x-1000x more productive, equivalent to teams of 50+ traditional developers
money_quotes: Joel Spolsky wrote one of the most useful pieces of software advice anyone has ever given... DON'T REWRITE YOUR SOFTWARE! And he was right! Outstanding essay. But unfortunately, not so timeless as we thought. It proved to have a shelf life of only about a quarter century.
Generating almost any code is easier (for AIs) than rewriting it. Hence, recreating software stacks from scratch is starting to become the new normal.

AI cognition takes a hit every time it crosses a boundary in the code. Every RPC, IPC, FFI call, database call... every single time the AI has to reason cognitively across a boundary or threshold, it gets a little dumber.
If you always take the first thing it generates, with no review at all, you're bound to be disappointed.
Reading code is by far the hardest part of coding. And with vibe coding, reading code is pretty much all you do all day.
Working with AIs is a little messy and nondeterministic. And I think that's here to stay.
Next year, you're going to have engineers who can build and maintain an entire company's software on their own.
posts.key_insight: The era of preserving code is ending - with AI agents, software becomes disposable and the skill that matters is orchestrating agents effectively through iterative review and swarming, not writing code yourself.

posts.summary: Yegge recounts how he skeptically investigated a claim that he had tens of thousands of dollars waiting for him on BAGS, a cryptocurrency trading platform for creators. Despite his lifelong distrust of 'free money' schemes stemming from Publisher's Clearing House and Nigerian Prince scams, he took the plunge and successfully claimed $68k-75k in trading fees generated by speculators betting on his Gas Town project. He argues BAGS represents something genuinely new: a market mechanism that channels speculation into fuel for independent creators, positioning it as the stock market equivalent for the coming AI-powered creator economy.
key_points: BAGS is a crypto market where speculators bet on creators, and 99% of trading fees go directly to the creator - turning speculation into innovation fuel
Unlike Bitcoin which has only rarity as value, BAGS is theoretically anchored to real-world creator output, similar to how stocks are tied to company performance
The AI-powered creator economy will soon dwarf the corporate economy, with individual creators using tools like Gas Town to rival company output
Traditional financial institutions (like Charles Schwab) are woefully unprepared for this new economy - crypto transactions were instant while banks required physical paper and 10-day waits
Yegge has bet his entire professional reputation on Gas Town and is grateful that BAGS is directly funding his open-source development
money_quotes: With AI, big software companies are facing an onslaught later this year: hordes of ravenous creators who have Gas Town and clusters of local GPUs.
One creator can make a product using Gas Town that would have taken a whole company before.
BAGS is a trading market targeted at predicting and fostering creators: individuals or maybe small teams who are out there creating amazing things.
There's always a deposit required to unlock that invisible magic free money. That's how you tell it's fake and you ain't gettin' shit.

The stock market is a great model: a strong, consensus-based signal of success for big corporations. We are going to need a market to do the same for the long tail, when it gets big enough to start wagging the dog.
Millions of independent creators will appear when everyone can vibe code, which is roughly 2 frontier-model upgrades from now.
It'll be a bloodbath like the old Los Angeles music industry when indie music and then social media hit.
posts.key_insight: Speculation markets like BAGS could become the stock market for the creator economy, channeling betting money into fuel for independent innovation at exactly the moment AI tools make individual creators as powerful as companies.

posts.summary: Yegge announces that his Gas Town orchestrator represents the future of coding: factory-style agent colonies rather than individual super-agents. He argues that coding agents will evolve from 'pair programmers' into 'colony workers' optimized for orchestration, and that this shift will devastate large companies while enabling tiny teams to achieve unprecedented productivity. The post traces Gas Town's evolution through four prior orchestrator attempts and explains why Go emerged as his preferred language for AI-coded projects. He predicts 2026 will see massive industry churn as solo developers and tiny teams dramatically outperform large organizations that can't adapt to the coordination challenges of high-velocity AI coding.
key_points: Nature prefers colonies over super-workers: the future isn't bigger coding agents but coordinated swarms of them working as factory workers
Big companies are fundamentally screwed because the 'form factor is wrong' - teams move so fast that even 2-hour-old information is 'ancient'
Go is surprisingly ideal for AI-coded projects because it's 'boring' and readable, whereas TypeScript wastes tokens on type manipulation
Coding agent vendors need to shift from building 'pair programmers' to building colony workers with proper orchestration APIs
The industry will explode into tiny companies before eventually re-aggregating, with solo developers potentially matching the output of large teams
money_quotes: When work needs to be done, nature prefers colonies. Nature builds ant colonies, while Claude Code is 'the world's biggest fuckin' ant.'
Colonies are going to win. Factories are going to win. Automation is going to win. Of course they're gonna fucking win. Anyone who thinks otherwise is, well, not a big fan of history, I guess.

The entire world is going to explode into tiny companies, which will then aggregate and re-form into larger ones… but not until we go through at least a year of churn, where small shops dramatically outperform large ones, to a degree we've never seen in all history.
2 hours ago!? That's ancient! It might as well have been 2 weeks ago.
At this rate I'll be more impressed if a 100-person startup can make a billion dollars in 2027. Genuinely impressed. How will they even communicate, with that many people doing AI work at once?
When the diffs go by in TypeScript, half the time you're like, what awful thing is my computer up to now? But with Go, it's just boring.
Programming has always been a best-effort, we'll-fix-shit-later endeavor. We always ship with bugs.
posts.key_insight: The future of software development isn't smarter individual coding agents but orchestrated colonies of them, which will fundamentally reshape the industry by making tiny teams vastly more productive than large organizations.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge introduces Gas Town, his fourth attempt at building an AI agent orchestrator that lets developers run 20-30 Claude Code instances simultaneously. Built on top of his Beads issue tracker, Gas Town uses a molecular workflow system (MEOW stack) where work is expressed as composable, persistent units backed by Git. The system features seven specialized worker roles including polecats (ephemeral workers), a Refinery (merge queue manager), and patrol agents that keep everything running. Yegge argues this represents the next evolution beyond single-agent coding, though he repeatedly warns that only developers already juggling 5+ AI agents daily should attempt to use it.
key_points: Gas Town orchestrates 20-30 Claude Code instances using persistent identities and workflows stored in Git via Beads
The MEOW stack (Molecular Expression of Work) enables durable, resumable workflows through 'Nondeterministic Idempotence' - work completes eventually as long as you keep throwing agents at it

Seven worker roles cooperate: Mayor (concierge), Polecats (ephemeral swarm workers), Refinery (merge queue), Witness (polecat monitor), Deacon (daemon), Dogs (helpers), and Crew (your personal agents)
GUPP (Gastown Universal Propulsion Principle) keeps agents working: if there's work on your hook, you must run it - though Claude Code's politeness often requires nudging
The system is deliberately chaotic and throughput-focused: some work gets lost, bugs get fixed multiple times, but you're 'flying' through enormous backlogs
money_quotes: Gas Town is an industrialized coding factory manned by superintelligent robot chimps, and when they feel like it, they can wreck your shit in an instant.
The industry is an embarrassing little kid's soccer team chasing the 2025 CLI form factor of Claude Code, rather than building what's next.
Work becomes fluid, an uncountable substance that you sling around freely, like slopping shiny fish into wooden barrels at the docks.
In Gas Town, an agent is not a session. Sessions are ephemeral; they are the 'cattle' in the Kubernetes 'pets vs cattle' metaphor.
I went everywhere, to everyone. 'It will be like kubernetes, but for agents,' I said. But hell, we couldn't even get people to use Claude Code, let alone think about using 10 to 20 of them at once.
K8s asks 'Is it running?' while Gas Town asks 'Is it done?'
I'm tired of walking around and telling people the future, just waving it right in their faces, and not being believed.
It's also 100% vibe coded. I've never seen the code, and I never care to.
posts.key_insight: The next frontier in AI-assisted development isn't better single agents but orchestrating swarms of them through persistent, Git-backed workflow systems that treat sessions as disposable cattle while work molecules remain durable.

posts.summary: Steve Yegge reflects on the explosive growth of agentic AI coding tools in early 2026, discussing his projects Gas Town and Beads while navigating VC interest and unexpected crypto windfalls. He describes how running 20+ AI agents simultaneously has become so cognitively demanding that it causes involuntary 'nap-strikes,' comparing the mental load to Jeff Bezos reviewing complex decisions all day. Yegge predicts 2026 will see individual engineers achieve 1000x-10000x productivity through agent orchestration, fundamentally transforming software development. He compares competing orchestrators (Ralph Wiggum, Loom, Claude Flow, Gas Town) and outlines his vision for Gas City, a toolkit for building custom orchestration systems.
key_points: Agentic coding at scale causes cognitive exhaustion severe enough to trigger involuntary naps—a phenomenon Yegge and colleagues compare to 'Jeff Bezos Mode' of processing complex decisions all day
The productivity gap between engineers using agent swarms versus those who aren't is approaching 1000x-10000x, with some already running 22+ Claude Pro Max subscriptions ($4400/month)

Different orchestrators solve different problems: Ralph handles quality through self-review loops, Loom coordinates teams, while Gas Town focuses on work tracking and audit trails via a blockchain-like ledger
Yegge is deliberately avoiding VC funding and crypto community demands to maintain focus on building toward his 'software orchestration trilogy'
2026 will see single engineers capable of cloning entire tech stacks (like Java/JVM) and small teams recreating enterprise systems in any language
money_quotes: 2026 is shaping up to be straight-up insane. There's a lot going on, and it's speeding up.
Money has a good nose, and it smells big changes in the air this year.
Your buffer just fills up and you're gone. I've fallen asleep slower at the anesthesiologist.
Our hypothesis is that we're operating at such a high level of decision-making that we're exhausting some internal buffers, and need to grab some gradient-descent time before we can continue.
With agentic coding, it's about to go to 1000x or even 10000x.
Everyone is going to learn how this stuff works this year, or die not trying. The industry won't be kind to the people who sleep on this.
I contend that Beads was like the discovery of oil. Right now everyone is playing with it and lighting lanterns, not realizing it's going to turn into the global petroleum industry for data.
Dario Amodei refers to 2026 as 'The Endgame.' Well, we're here.
posts.key_insight: Running swarms of AI coding agents has become so cognitively intense that it triggers involuntary sleep, signaling we've entered an era where individual engineers can achieve previously impossible productivity—but only if they learn to orchestrate agents effectively.
